properties.name: Hyde Park Barracks
properties.summary: Greenway's convict barracks: dormitory, immigration depot, asylum, courthouse, museum.

timeline.event: Designed by convict architect Francis Greenway, the Hyde Park Barracks are opened as a solution for controlling convicts after hours. It was built to house up to 600 men, but at times 1400 men lived here.

The Hyde Park Barracks became a place of secondary punishment and a depot for reassignment and trial. A court is established to adminster punishments for government-employed convicts, with penalties including solitary confiment, walking the treadmill, or up to 150 lashes.

With the cessation of transportation as policy, the Barracks is closed as convict accomodation, with 8000 men having passed through it. Instead, it becomes a female immigration depot — the first roof in Australia for thousands of 'unprotected females', including the Irish Famine orphans.
The top floor of the main building was converted into an asylum, providing care and lodgings for aged, destitute, terminally ill and mentally ill women, who lived out the rest of their days here.

In the 1880s overcrowding was a constant problem, and by 1886 the Hyde Park Asylum is closed. By the following year, the barracks, renamed as Chancery Square, is converted into a complex of law courts and government offices. The expansive dormitories are subbdivided into offices, and new fibro buildings are constructed, housing the offices of numerous government agencies including the Supreme Court Judges, Arbitration and Bankruptcy Courts.

Extensive conservation works begin, with the main building restored to its original appearance. Over 100,000 personal items are recovered from beneath the floorboards, left behind by those who passed through. In 1990, the barracks are opened as a museum, housing one of the most extensive archaeological collections in Australia.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List among the Australian Convict Sites.

stories: We have rats to thank for the Barracks' impressive museum collection! Much of the historical material was dragged under the floorboards by rats, scurrying, stealing and collecting rubbish to make their nests. Thanks to the rats, we have an incredible insight into how these men and women lived.

A few barracks convicts achieved fame or notoriety, including, along with architect Francis Greenway, poet Francis Macnamara, bushrangers Lawrence Kavanagh and William Westwood, murderer John Knatchbull, Khoi chief David Stuurman, writer Thomas Cook, potter John Moreton and hangman Alexander Green.

With the end of convict transportion, there was a major gender imbalance in the colony. As a result, the government introduced schemes to import women into the colony, many of which were orphans from the crowded workhouses of famine-plagued Ireland. Over 2,000 of these girls passed through Hyde Park Barracks, immortalised through a memorial built into the southern wall of the museum.

During its time as a complex of law offices and courts, two landmark legal decisions were made at the site: in 1927 the basic living wage was approved, and in 1921 a case for equal pay for women was presented and rejected, but not granted until 1973.
properties.image_caption: Hyde Park Barracks on Macquarie Street
properties.source: Museums of History NSW; UNESCO

properties.name: The Mint (Rum Hospital)
properties.current_use: Museums of History NSW head office
properties.summary: The Rum Hospital's southern wing, later the Royal Mint's first overseas branch.

timeline.event: Opens as the southern wing of the General Hospital, used initially as accomodation for assistant surgeons, and later as a dispensary and infirmary for the poor.

After the gold rushes, becomes the Royal Mint's first branch outside London. In the first five years, over a million pounds worth of gold were converted into sovereign and half-sovereign coins each year. In 1868, Sydney's coins were recognised as legal tender in all British colonies, but it was not until 1886 that they were accepted in Britain.

The Mint closes due to ageing equipment and unprofitability. A series of government departments sought office space in the building, rotating in and out as they occupied the old factory buildings and its forecourt was transformed into a carpark.
Restored as the headquarters of the Historic Houses Trust, now Museums of History NSW.

stories: The contractors built the hospital for free in exchange for the right to import 45,000 gallons of spirits.

Colonial Architect Francis Greenway's official report on the workmanship heavily criticised the building's quality. Declaring that the foundations weak and that had been cut during construction, it is quite impressive that the Mint is the oldest surviving public building in Sydney.
The Mint's gates, removed in the 1930s to make way for cars, were acquired by Barker College at Hornsby in 1937.
The inspiration for the form of the buildings is thought to have come from Macquarie's time in India, especially the Madras Government House.
properties.image_caption: The Mint in 1870 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Museums of History NSW; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: St James' Church
properties.summary: Begun as a courthouse, consecrated in 1824 — the oldest surviving church in Sydney.

timeline.event: Construction begins, designed by Francis Greenway and intended as the first purpose-built courthouse of the colony.
Commissioner Bigge cancels Macquarie's 'grand metropolitan church' plans on George Street, and so the half-built courthouse is converted into a church instead.
The first service is held at the unfinished (and unconsecrated) St James for convicts housed at Hyde Park Barracks.
Consecrated as St James Church.

Beneath the Church, a vaulted undercroft called the crypt is expanded to include the Children's Chapel, its ceiling decorated by the Turramurra painters with biblical scenes re-set in Sydney Harbour, ringed by local animals and flowers.

stories: For the first 50 years of its life, St James Church was the tallest structure in all of Sydney at 52m tall, finally overtaken in 1879 by the ill-fated Garden Palace. The latter's fiery destruction in 1882 saw St James re-gain the crown briefly, but it was once again snatched in 1889, this time by the towering Sydney Town Hall. As such, for decades the copper-sheathed spire was used as a navigation mark, with ships entering the Heads lined up on Greenway's steeple.
Convicts and society worshipped under one roof, strictly segregated; the prisoners sat in galleries above the gentry's box pews.
properties.image_caption: The Supreme Court and St James' spire in 1842
properties.source: St James' King Street; Dictionary of Sydney, Our History - St. James

properties.name: Elizabeth Bay House
properties.original_use: Residence of Colonial Secretary Alexander Macleay
properties.summary: 'The finest house in the colony' — and a financial ruin for the man who built it.

timeline.event: Governor Darling grants newly arrived NSW Colonial Secretary Alexander Macleay with 54 acres at Elizabeth Bay. Using assigned convict labour and assisted by gardener Robert Henderson, Macleay establishes a private botanic garden with picturesque features of dwarf stone walls, rustic bridges, and winding gravel walks.
John Verge designs a Greek-revival villa for Alexander Macleay, set in a famous 54-acre harbourside garden.
The Macleays move in.
Depression debts force Macleay out, and his eldest son takes over the house. It is passed through the family until 1903, when the last Macleays move out.
Divided into fifteen flats.
Opens as a house museum.

stories: The oval saloon, its cantilevered staircase spiralling beneath a glass dome, is routinely called the finest colonial interior in Australia.

Famous for his keen interest in entymology, Macleay arrived in Sydney in 1826 with the finest and most extensive insect collection of any private indivdual. Transferred to the Macleay Museum at the University of Sydney in 1890, it became the foundation of Australia's natural history collection.
properties.image_caption: Elizabeth Bay House — 'the finest house in the colony'
properties.source: Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Government House
properties.summary: A Gothic-revival castle designed in London by an architect who never saw Sydney.

timeline.event: Construction begins to Royal Architect Edward Blore's London-drawn design.
Governor George Gipps moves in.
Hosts Australia's first Governor-General as the Commonwealth is proclaimed.
The building once again serves as the residence, office and official reception space for the Governor of New South Wales.

Under the direction of NSW Premier Bob Carr, the Governor moves out and the house opens fully to the public. As for Carr's reasoning, he stated: "The Office of the Governor should be less associated with pomp and ceremony, less encumbered by anachronistic protocol, more in tune with the character of the people."
The Governor of NSW returns to residence.

stories: It is among the oldest continuously used government houses in the world, still hosting swearings-in beneath its original state rooms.

Rivalry between New South Wales and Victoria led the Commonwealth Government to designate Government House in Sydney and Melbourne Government House as official residences of equal status for the Governor-General.
properties.image_caption: Government House from its gardens
properties.source: Governor of NSW; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Customs House
properties.architect: Mortimer Lewis; rebuilt by James Barnet
properties.summary: For over 150 years, Customs House played an active role as the primary trade gateway for goods and people flowing into Sydney and Australia.

timeline.event: Mortimer Lewis's two-storey customs house opens on the waterfront of Circular Quay. The jagged line of dark granite in the square marks the original highwater mark.
James Barnet significantly expands Customs House. He adds a grander portico entrance, an extra floor, a clock and 2 wings along the sides.
Walter Liberty Vernon adds further floors as trade swells.
The Customs Service moves out after 145 years.
Reopens as the city's flagship public library.

stories: The 16 black swastikas decorating the terrazzo floor in Customs House foyer were selected by the architect George Oakeshott during a major reconstruction of the building in 1915 to 1917 as classical ornamentation. Associated with the Nazis today, these symbols were symbols of peace and prosperity for many cultures across the world.
Under the lobby's glass floor lies a detailed scale model of the city.
properties.image_caption: Customs House around 1900
properties.source: City of Sydney; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sydney Town Hall
properties.current_use: Seat of the City of Sydney
properties.summary: This sandstone confection rose over the Old Sydney Burial Ground, the colony's first cemetery, where about 2,200 people were laid to rest between 1792 and 1820. The building took two decades to finish.

timeline.event: Prince Alfred, the Duke of Edinburgh, laid the foundation stone on the old George Street burial ground during the first royal visit to the colony.
The Centennial Hall opened, drawing the long building campaign to a close after roof girders shipped from England held things up.
The Grand Organ was installed by William Hill and Son of London, and it was then the largest in the world.
Archaeologists working beneath the floor uncovered dozens of graves that the Victorian builders had missed.

stories: 'Meet me on the Town Hall steps' has been Sydney's default rendezvous for more than a century.
The graves were largely exhumed when the hall was built in the 1880s and the remains reinterred at Rookwood, but not all: the 2008 works still turned up burials missed beneath the floor.
The Grand Organ's full-length 64-foot pipes still place it among the largest playable instruments on earth.
properties.image_caption: Sydney Town Hall and its clock tower
properties.source: City of Sydney

properties.name: General Post Office
properties.current_use: Hotel, dining and offices, No. 1 Martin Place
properties.summary: James Barnet's sandstone palace of the mails was the grandest building in the colony when it opened, and it still straddles the buried Tank Stream that once watered the settlement.

timeline.event: The first stage opened on 1 September, a vast sandstone post office facing what would become Martin Place, with 1,500 guests at the ceremony.
Tomaso Sani's Pitt Street carvings of ordinary postmen and tradespeople were denounced in Parliament as crude caricatures. Critics called them 'Barnet's blot', but Barnet refused to remove them.
The clock tower finally rose over Martin Place, and Lady Jersey set its works ticking in September.
Fearing the tower would guide Japanese bombers, workers took it down stone by stone and put it into storage.
The tower went back up after twenty-two years in pieces.
Australia Post leased the building to private operators for 99 years, and the hotel and dining arcade followed in 1999.

stories: Look up on the Pitt Street side: the 'scandalous' carvings of postmen, blacksmiths and merchants are all still there. Barnet won.
The building straddles the Tank Stream, which still runs in its brick tunnel beneath the foundations.
properties.image_caption: The General Post Office on Martin Place
properties.source: City of Sydney; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Queen Victoria Building
properties.original_use: City produce markets and concert hall
properties.summary: George McRae's Romanesque market hall went up through the 1890s depression, partly to keep stonemasons and craftsmen in work. A century later it came within a whisker of being knocked down for a car park.

timeline.event: The building opened as the Queen Victoria Markets, with domes and stained glass rising above the produce stalls.
As the markets faded, the interior was carved up and the city library moved into the old concert hall, staying for decades.
An alderman proposed pulling the building down, and schemes for a civic square and car park circled for the next dozen years.
After a full restoration, the building reopened as the shopping arcade we know today.

stories: A sealed letter from Queen Elizabeth II hangs inside, addressed to the Lord Mayor of Sydney in 2085, nobody alive knows what it says.
Through the day the Royal Clock cycles six royal scenes, among them the beheading of Charles I, staged above the heads of unsuspecting shoppers.
properties.image_caption: The Queen Victoria Markets in 1917
properties.source: QVB; City of Sydney

properties.name: St Mary's Cathedral
properties.summary: Australia's longest church took the better part of two centuries to complete. William Wardell drew the design, but the twin southern spires he imagined were only finished in 2000.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie laid the foundation stone of the first St Mary's, on the earliest Catholic chapel site in the country.
Fire tore through the first cathedral on a winter night and destroyed it.
Archbishop Polding laid the stone for William Wardell's Gothic Revival replacement.
Archbishop Vaughan dedicated the first completed section for worship in September.
The nave was finished in time for the International Eucharistic Congress.
The twin southern spires were lowered into place by helicopter and clad in Gosford sandstone, 132 years after building began.

stories: The crypt floor by the Melocco brothers took some thirteen years, a Celtic illuminated manuscript rendered entirely in terrazzo.
Wardell was asked for 'a cathedral for the ages' and never saw it finished. Neither did any architect who worked on it.
properties.image_caption: St Mary's Cathedral, the spires arrived in 2000
properties.source: St Mary's Cathedral; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Central Railway Station
properties.summary: Sydney's main railway station stands on the old Devonshire Street cemetery, cleared of its dead to make way for the platforms. The clock tower arrived fifteen years after the station opened.

timeline.event: The Devonshire Street cemetery was consecrated on the sand hills here, soon nicknamed the Sandhills Cemetery.
Around 30,000 burials were exhumed and carried out to suburban cemeteries to clear the ground for the railway.
The new station opened in August, replacing the cramped terminus that had stood just to the south.
The clock tower, rising about 85 metres, finally opened at the north-western corner.
Electric services were extended underground to Museum and St James, knitting the suburbs into the city.

stories: Families could claim their dead for reburial; unclaimed remains went to Botany. Workmen turned up forgotten graves for decades afterwards.
Just west stands the Gothic Mortuary Station (1869), where funeral trains departed for Rookwood Necropolis, mourners and coffins ticketed on the same train.
properties.image_caption: The first train leaves the new Central Station, 1906
properties.source: Sydney Trains; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Bondi Pavilion
properties.original_use: Bathing pavilion, Turkish baths, ballroom, changing rooms for thousands
properties.summary: Bondi Pavilion opened in 1929 as a grand inter-war Mediterranean bathing palace, with Turkish baths, a ballroom and changing rooms built to handle crowds in the thousands. For nearly a century it has been the social heart of the beach.

timeline.event: The Pavilion opens in December with Turkish baths, a ballroom, shops and changing rooms on a scale meant for huge summer crowds.
During the war it hosts dances for servicemen, while the beach itself is strung with barbed wire against invasion.
Prime Minister Gough Whitlam opens the old ballroom as a community theatre. By 1978 the whole building has been reborn as a community arts centre.
The Pavilion reopens after a major restoration that revived its 1920s character.

stories: Beach inspector Aub Laidlaw policed swimsuits from here for decades, measuring costumes and marching bikini wearers off the sand as 'indecent' well into the 1960s.
Between the courtyard cinema, the bands and the festivals, the Pav has served as the eastern suburbs' living room for nearly a century.
properties.image_caption: Bondi Pavilion soon after opening, c. 1930
properties.source: Waverley Council

properties.name: Sydney Harbour Bridge
properties.current_use: Road, rail and foot crossing, plus the BridgeClimb
properties.summary: Sydneysiders call it the coathanger. The harbour was crossed only after a century of failed schemes, then nine years of work that put a single great steel arch over the water. The opening in 1932 was famously upstaged by a man on a horse.

timeline.event: Convict architect Francis Greenway is already urging a bridge across the harbour, more than a century before one is built.
The contract goes to the English firm Dorman Long in March. Hundreds of houses in The Rocks and North Sydney are pulled down and their tenants pushed out with no compensation.
The two half-arches, each built outward from its own shore, swing together and meet over the middle of the harbour.
On 19 March, just as Premier Jack Lang prepares to cut the ribbon, Captain Francis de Groot of the far-right New Guard rides forward and slashes it first with a cavalry sword.
BridgeClimb opens, letting paying visitors walk to the top of the arch.

stories: Sixteen workers died building it. The riveters and 'spidermen' worked the bare steel without harnesses; one survivor of a fall, Vincent Kelly, reportedly broke ribs but swam ashore and returned to work.
Nine-year-old Lennie Gwyther rode his pony Ginger Mick roughly 1,000 km from country Victoria, alone, to watch the bridge open, and became a national celebrity.
Decades later a young rigger on the bridge maintenance gangs, Paul Hogan, won his break on a TV talent show, and went from painting steel to Crocodile Dundee.
properties.image_caption: The completed arch from the north shore, 1930 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Bridge records (NSW State Archives); Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sydney Opera House
properties.current_use: Performing arts centre, UNESCO World Heritage
properties.summary: Jorn Utzon won an international competition in 1957 with a sketch so daring that engineers spent years working out how to build it at all, and the shells took sixteen years to rise from idea to opening night. The final bill came to roughly fourteen times the early estimate, paid for not by taxes but by a public lottery that ran for decades. Utzon resigned in 1966 and never saw the finished building, which is the quiet ache at the heart of one of the world's most loved structures.

timeline.event: Utzon's entry wins the international competition. By a well-worn account, judge Eero Saarinen pulled the design back out of the rejected pile and insisted it was the only true masterpiece among the 233 submissions.

Building starts on Bennelong Point under political pressure to begin, well before anyone has solved how to actually construct the soaring shells. Funding came not from the public purse but from the Opera House Lotteries, which would eventually sell tens of millions of tickets.

After years of failed schemes, Utzon finds the answer in a simple piece of geometry: cut every shell from the surface of one sphere. Suddenly the roof can be standardised, prefabricated, and actually built.

Starved of fees and stripped of control by a new state government, Utzon resigns in February. He leaves Australia within weeks and never returns, even when the building he imagined becomes world famous.

Queen Elizabeth II opens the building on 20 October. The final cost is $102 million against the original $7 million estimate, almost all of it raised ticket by ticket through the lotteries. Utzon is not invited and is not mentioned in the speeches.
The Opera House is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site. Utzon, then 89, is still alive, one of the rare architects to see their own work listed, though he never did walk through its doors.

stories: It was paid for not from taxes but by the Opera House Lotteries, Sydney gambled its way to a masterpiece.
The first unofficial performance came in 1960, when Paul Robeson climbed the scaffolding and sang 'Ol' Man River' to the construction workers at lunch.
Utzon never set eyes on the finished building, but the reconciliation came late: he rejoined as design consultant in 1999, and the Utzon Room was named while he could still hear of it.
properties.image_caption: The shells rising over Bennelong Point, mid-1960s
properties.source: Sydney Opera House; UNESCO; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Old Government House
properties.architect: rebuilt under Governor Hunter; Greenway porch 1816
properties.summary: Old Government House is the oldest surviving public building in Australia, standing on the rise above the Parramatta River where the colony's farms first took hold. For the early governors it was a country seat that often mattered more than Sydney itself.

timeline.event: Within months of the First Fleet's arrival, Governor Phillip sets up a farming settlement here at Rose Hill, and its crops help keep the hungry colony alive.
Governor Hunter clears away Phillip's cottage and builds a more permanent brick residence on the same spot. That brick core still stands inside the house today.
The house is enlarged into an elegant Palladian residence during Macquarie's time, with a portico attributed to the convict architect Francis Greenway.
Governors stop living here, and the house passes through a run of schools and tenants.
The National Trust begins restoring it as a museum.
It is inscribed on the World Heritage list as one of eleven Australian Convict Sites.

stories: Ten of the first eleven governors used this as their country house, for several it was effectively the capital, since Parramatta's farms mattered more than Sydney's harbour.
It is the oldest surviving public building on the Australian mainland, older than anything in Macquarie Street.
properties.image_caption: Old Government House, Parramatta Park
properties.source: National Trust (NSW); UNESCO

properties.name: Elizabeth Farm
properties.summary: Elizabeth Farm is the oldest surviving European dwelling in Australia, the early walls hidden inside a later bungalow. It was the home and headquarters of the Macarthurs, who turned colonial New South Wales into a wool country.

timeline.event: A four-room brick cottage rises on the Macarthurs' Rose Hill grant. Parts of those first walls still survive within the house.
John Macarthur is sent into exile after the Rum Rebellion. For close to a decade Elizabeth runs the merino flocks herself, so the early wool story is as much hers as his.
The house is remodelled into the elegant low bungalow, with its wide Doric verandah, that visitors see today.
After almost ninety years the estate is sold out of the Macarthur family and broken up.
William Swann buys the run-down house and its land for £600 and sets about restoring it. His family keep it largely unchanged until 1968.
It opens as a museum without barriers, where visitors can sit on the furniture and open the doors for themselves.

stories: The merino gamble that built Australia's wool trade was managed from this verandah, by Elizabeth, while John was exiled or in England for eleven of the critical years.
It's a museum without barriers: you can lie on the daybed, open the shutters and read at the table, in the oldest house in the country.
properties.image_caption: Elizabeth Farm, Rosehill
properties.source: Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Anzac Memorial
properties.architect: C. Bruce Dellit; sculpture by Rayner Hoff
properties.original_use: Memorial to NSW's First World War service
properties.summary: The Anzac Memorial is Sydney's art deco monument to grief rather than glory. Its dome holds about 120,000 gold stars, one for every man and woman from New South Wales who enlisted in the First World War, and at its heart lies Rayner Hoff's bronze of a fallen soldier.

timeline.event: A public fund opens on the first anniversary of the Gallipoli landing to pay for a memorial.

C. Bruce Dellit's art deco tower is dedicated. Its dome is set with around 120,000 gold stars, one for each person from New South Wales who enlisted, many of them sold to the public for two shillings to finish the building.
The memorial is rededicated to all Australians who have served in war.
The Centenary Extension opens, adding a hall that holds soil gathered from the home towns of the state's First World War volunteers.

stories: Beneath the dome lies Hoff's bronze 'Sacrifice', a naked dead soldier carried on a shield. You look down on him from the gallery: mourning, not triumph.
Two more Hoff sculptures were never installed, his 'Crucifixion of Civilisation', a nude female Peace on a cross, was denounced by churchmen as blasphemous and quietly dropped.
properties.image_caption: The Anzac Memorial, Hyde Park South
properties.source: Anzac Memorial Trustees; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: University of Sydney Quadrangle
properties.summary: Edmund Blacket gave the young University of Sydney a Gothic quadrangle in honey-coloured sandstone, a colonial answer to Oxford and Cambridge on a hill above the city.

timeline.event: The University of Sydney is founded by act of the colonial parliament, the first university in Australia and, in principle, open to students of any religion.
Blacket's Great Hall is completed and the first degrees are conferred in it that July. His opening range of the quadrangle follows, finished by 1862.
The university admits women to its degrees, putting it among the earliest in the world to do so.
The war memorial carillon is dedicated on Anzac Day and rings out over the quad. The famous jacaranda is planted in the south-east corner the same year.

stories: Student superstition holds that if you haven't started studying by the time the quad's jacaranda blooms in November, it's already too late. When the original tree fell in 2016, the university planted a graft of it, the superstition survives.
Blacket carved a colonial Oxford complete with gargoyles, look for distinctly Australian fauna among the grotesques.
properties.image_caption: Blacket's Main Quadrangle, University of Sydney
properties.source: University of Sydney archives; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sydney Hospital
properties.original_use: The colony's hospital, successor to the 1788 tent hospital
properties.summary: Australia's oldest hospital traces its line from First Fleet tents in 1788 to the grand sandstone building on Macquarie Street, and to the brass boar out front whose snout is rubbed for luck.

timeline.event: The colony's first hospital opens in tents at The Rocks within weeks of the First Fleet landing.
The 'Rum Hospital' is completed on Macquarie Street, paid for by giving its builders a monopoly on importing spirits. This site holds its central wing.

Lucy Osburn and five Nightingale-trained nurses arrive from London, sent by Florence Nightingale after an appeal from Premier Henry Parkes. Within days they are nursing the wounded Prince Alfred, and modern Australian nursing takes root here.
A grand new sandstone hospital opens to replace the crumbling Rum Hospital wing. Thomas Rowe had won the competition, but John Kirkpatrick finished the job.
Il Porcellino, a bronze boar cast from Florence's famous one, is installed out front. Coins dropped at his feet still go to the hospital.

stories: Lucy Osburn's nurses arrived one week before O'Farrell shot Prince Alfred at Clontarf, nursing the royal patient made their reputation overnight and rewrote Australian hospital care.
Il Porcellino's snout is polished gold by a million hopeful hands; the coins dropped at his feet still go to the hospital.
properties.image_caption: Sydney Hospital on Macquarie Street
properties.source: Sydney Hospital heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: State Theatre
properties.current_use: Live theatre, home of the Sydney Film Festival
properties.summary: Billed on opening as 'the Empire's greatest theatre', the State is a 1929 picture palace that piles Gothic vaulting, baroque marble and Hollywood swagger into one room.

timeline.event: The theatre opens in June with around 2,700 seats, mixing Gothic vaults, baroque marble and bronze under a coffered dome.
Its Koh-i-Noor cut-crystal chandelier, weighing more than four tonnes, hangs over the Grand Assembly. It was reckoned the second largest in the world.
The State becomes home to the Sydney Film Festival, which still fills it every June.
Heritage protection ends years of demolition threat that had already claimed its rivals, the Regent, the Prince Edward and the Trocadero.

stories: Of the great inter-war picture palaces Sydney built and then bulldozed, the State is essentially the only survivor, step inside and you're in the cinema-going 1930s.
The basement once held a ballroom and a tiled 'Pompeii' smoking room; the building was designed so a patron need never stop being amazed between the street and the screen.
properties.image_caption: The State Theatre illuminated, early 1930s (Sam Hood)
properties.source: State Theatre heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Australia Square
properties.architect: Harry Seidler with Pier Luigi Nervi
properties.summary: Harry Seidler's round tower announced the arrival of high-rise Sydney, rising over a block of small buildings that were swept away to make room for it.

timeline.event: A 150-foot height limit, set partly by the reach of fire ladders, caps Sydney's buildings for the next 45 years.
The limit is lifted and the race to build tall begins.
Seidler's 50-storey circular tower opens, then the world's tallest lightweight-concrete building, engineered with Pier Luigi Nervi. Around thirty buildings were demolished to clear the block.
The plaza gains its Alexander Calder sculpture and, for a time, a Le Corbusier tapestry, modernism offered as a public gift.

stories: The revolving restaurant on level 47 gave a generation of Sydneysiders their first aerial view of their own city, a slow lap every hour and three-quarters.
Seidler fought for the open plaza when plot ratios rewarded filling the block, the bet that public space would pay became the model for every tower forecourt since.
properties.image_caption: Seidler's round tower at Australia Square
properties.source: Seidler Architectural Foundation; City of Sydney

properties.name: Darlinghurst Gaol
properties.summary: For more than seventy years this was Sydney's main gaol, its cell blocks fanning out from a central rotunda and its gallows taking close to eighty lives. Since the 1920s the same sandstone has held art students instead of prisoners.

timeline.event: Convict gangs begin the great sandstone perimeter wall along the line Francis Greenway pegged out, and then the work stalls for more than a decade.
The radial gaol designed by Mortimer Lewis and Captain George Barney opens on the hill above the city, and prisoners are walked up in chains from the old George Street gaol.
The last public hanging takes place outside the gates. After this the gallows inside the walls do the work, and around 76 to 79 people die there over the gaol's lifetime.
Henry Lawson is locked up here several times between 1905 and 1910 for unpaid maintenance, and turns the place into verse as 'Starvinghurst'.
The prisoners are moved out to Long Bay and the gaol closes, serving briefly as a military detention barracks during the First World War.
The empty gaol passes to the Education Department, and East Sydney Technical College opens in the cell blocks the following year.
After years of lobbying, the National Art School wins independence from TAFE and makes the old gaol its own, with easels in the cells and the drawing studio in the circular chapel.

stories: Bushranger Captain Moonlite and the Mount Rennie convictions died on the gallows here; the morning crowds on Darlinghurst Road could hear the drop.
Henry Lawson, gaoled over unpaid maintenance, called it 'Starvinghurst Gaol' in verse, the name stuck among inmates.
Art students have now occupied the gaol longer than many of its sentences ran; the circular chapel is the school's drawing studio.
properties.image_caption: Darlinghurst Gaol
properties.source: National Art School heritage; NSW State Archives

properties.name: Female Orphan School
properties.summary: This is the oldest three-storey building in Australia, raised on the riverbank to house and school the colony's parentless girls. Elizabeth Macquarie chose its design, modelling it on Airds House, her own childhood home in Scotland.

timeline.event: Governor King opens the colony's first Female Orphan School in town, meant as much to clear girls off the streets as to teach them.
After building work that began in 1813, the first girls move into the grand new school on the river at Rydalmere.
The school becomes part of the Protestant Orphan School, and decades of institutional routine follow.
The building turns to medicine, taken over for psychiatric patients and soon known as Rydalmere, an asylum it would house for a century.
Restored in stages by Western Sydney University from 2000, the building reopens as the home of the Whitlam Institute.

stories: Girls entered at five and left at thirteen, apprenticed into service; the building's registers are a roll-call of the colony's broken families.
It is the oldest three-storey building in the country, built when the colony was barely thirty years old, on a river then busier than the roads.
properties.image_caption: The Female Orphan School above the Parramatta River
properties.source: Western Sydney University heritage; Female Orphan School records, NSW State Archives

properties.name: Strand Arcade
properties.current_use: Shopping arcade, the last Victorian arcade left
properties.summary: The Strand is the last of the five glass-roofed arcades that Victorian Sydney built between Pitt and George Streets. It opened in 1892 with electric light and hydraulic lifts, and it has outlived every one of its rivals.

timeline.event: The arcade opens on 1 April, the fifth and last of the city's Victorian arcades, fitted out with electric light, hydraulic lifts and a glass roof made for Sydney's sun.
Fire breaks out on the morning of 25 May and wrecks the George Street end, in a city that had already pulled down every sibling arcade.
Restoration is chosen over demolition. With the original drawings lost, a single surviving cedar shopfront becomes the template for copying the galleries and balustrades by 1978.

stories: Of the great arcades, the Royal, the Imperial, the Victoria, only the Strand survives; walking it is the last intact errand of Victorian shopping.
Its upper galleries kept Sydney's watchmakers, milliners and tailors through every retail age since the gaslight era.
properties.image_caption: The Strand's galleries, essentially unchanged since 1892
properties.source: Strand Arcade heritage; City of Sydney

properties.name: Hornby Lighthouse
properties.summary: Hornby Light was thrown up in less than a year after two terrible wrecks at the harbour mouth, marking the true entrance that the Dunbar had missed in the dark. Its red and white stripes have made it the most photographed of Sydney's small lighthouses.

timeline.event: The Dunbar is smashed beneath The Gap in August and the Catherine Adamson is lost at North Head in October, taking 121 and 21 lives between them.
Within the year the colony builds Hornby Light right on the tip of South Head, designed by Alexander Dawson and opened by Governor Denison, named for his wife's family.
The light is electrified and its keepers withdrawn, though their sandstone cottages still stand beside the tower.
Restored with its bold red and white vertical stripes back in place, the harbour's best loved little lighthouse.

stories: Its stripes weren't decoration: they made the tower unmistakable by day against the sandstone headland, just as the light marked it by night.
Together with the signal station and Macquarie Lighthouse up the ridge, South Head carries three generations of the harbour's eyes within one walk.
properties.image_caption: Hornby Light with the Lass o' Gowrie passing, 1880s (City of Sydney Archives)
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Sydney Harbour Federation Trust

properties.name: Barrenjoey Lighthouse
properties.original_use: Broken Bay light and customs lookout
properties.summary: James Barnet's golden sandstone tower stands on the headland at the city's northern edge, where Pittwater meets Broken Bay. It was the last and grandest of several lights raised here to guide ships past what smugglers had long treated as Sydney's back door.

timeline.event: A customs station goes up at the foot of the head to watch for ships slipping into Broken Bay and the Pittwater without reporting.
Two wooden beacons known as the Stewart Towers are built at either end of the head, replacing the rough basket fire that had burned since 1855.
Barnet's sandstone lighthouse, quarried on the spot, is first lit on 1 August, with George Mulhall, keeper here since 1868, in charge.
The light is converted to automatic operation and the keepers leave for good.
It still flashes every ten seconds over Pittwater, reached only on foot up the headland track, one of the best short walks in Sydney.

stories: First keeper George Mulhall died, the story insists, struck by lightning while gathering firewood, and lies buried with his wife just below the tower he lit.
The headland is Garigal country named Barrenjoey, 'young kangaroo', and the midden-rich tombolo connecting it to Palm Beach has fed people for millennia.
properties.image_caption: Barnet's sandstone tower on Barrenjoey Head
properties.source: NSW National Parks; Northern Beaches Council heritage

properties.name: Sydney Cricket Ground
properties.summary: The Sydney Cricket Ground began as an army paddock and grew into the most storied cricket ground in the country, famous as much for the roar from the old Hill as for the play on the square.

timeline.event: The Garrison Ground opens, levelled by soldiers of the 11th North Devonshire Regiment on land set aside for the army.
John Kirkpatrick's Members Stand goes up, a graceful piece of Victorian cricket architecture that still stands today.
The ground takes its modern name, and the great grass mound at the Randwick end is christened the Hill, the cheapest and rowdiest seats in the game.
On 30 January the young Don Bradman makes 134 not out against Victoria, his first century at the ground he would go on to dominate.
Stephen 'Yabba' Gascoigne, the Hill's most famous barracker, dies in January. A bronze of him would later be set where he once heckled.
World Series Cricket brings the floodlights. On 28 November the first night match at a traditional ground packs the SCG.

stories: The Hill is gone under grandstands, but its patron saint survives: Stephen 'Yabba' Gascoigne, the Balmain rabbito whose heckles ('Send 'im down a piano, see if 'e can play that!') are the founding texts of Australian barracking.
Trumper's leap and Bodyline's fury both happened on this square, the ground is the connective tissue of this map's cricket stories.
properties.image_caption: The 1886 Members Pavilion, cricket's prettiest grandstand
properties.source: SCG Trust heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Coogee Palace Aquarium
properties.summary: The Coogee Palace was a Victorian pleasure dome by the beach, holding an aquarium, swimming baths, a skating rink and a dance hall under one great glass roof. It later became the unlikely setting for one of Sydney's strangest murder cases.

timeline.event: The Palace opens on 23 December under its 55-foot glass dome, with an aquarium, swimming baths, a skating rink and a dance hall all under cover.
A captured tiger shark in the Palace pool brings up a tattooed human arm in front of the crowd, and the Shark Arm Case begins under this roof.
After decades of slow decline the grand old dome is sliding towards demolition.
A winter storm in June tears the top off the building and the great dome comes down.
The Palace is rebuilt to its Victorian design and the dome returns, a faithful copy of the original.
It reopens as the Coogee Pavilion, the dome now shading a giant scrabble board for the beachgoers below.

stories: The Shark Arm Case's first witness was a crowd of holidaymakers watching the aquarium pool, the most public start a murder investigation ever had.
Victorian Sydney built pleasure palaces at its beaches the way it built banks in town: domes, towers and total confidence.
properties.image_caption: The Palace's restored dome, now the Coogee Pavilion
properties.source: Randwick City Library local history; Coogee Pavilion heritage

properties.name: Archibald Fountain
properties.summary: A gift to Sydney from the editor who loved France, paying tribute to the French and Australian soldiers who fought side by side in the Great War. The bronze gods at its centre were modelled in Paris by a sculptor who never set eyes on the city they now command.

timeline.event: J.F. Archibald dies. The founding editor of the Bulletin and a devoted francophile, he leaves money in his will for an open-air memorial to the French and Australian alliance of the First World War, to be made by a French artist.
In Paris, the sculptor Francois-Leon Sicard finishes Apollo and his attendant figures in bronze, working from photographs of Hyde Park that he had been sent across the world.
The fountain is unveiled at the northern end of Hyde Park on 14 March by the Lord Mayor, and Sydney quickly adopts it as the place to meet a friend in town.

stories: Archibald's two bequests shaped Sydney culture from the grave: the portrait prize that scandalises annually, and the fountain everyone has agreed to meet at since 1932.
Sicard worked from photographs and never crossed the world to see his masterpiece in place; Apollo faces down Macquarie Street regardless.
properties.image_caption: Sicard's Apollo above the Archibald Fountain
properties.source: City of Sydney; Art Gallery of NSW, Archibald bequest

properties.name: Education Building
properties.summary: A grand sandstone office block raised for a bold idea, that the state owed every child a school. It was built to house the bureaucracy that ran public education across New South Wales.

timeline.event: The Public Instruction Act makes schooling compulsory and secular across the colony, and the government needs a large department to run it. Free schooling followed in 1906.

George McRae's Edwardian Baroque sandstone block on Bridge Street, occupied since September 1912, is finished. It became the administrative engine of public schooling, with its southern half added around 1938.
During the influenza emergency, clerks are photographed in masks at its entrance, the image shown on this card.

stories: Parkes's school system, Bridge Street's sandstone row, and the QVB share an author's lineage, McRae inherited Barnet's mantle and the city's confidence.
Together with the Lands and Chief Secretary's buildings, it makes Bridge Street the best Victorian-to-Federation streetscape in the country, the colony showing off its government.
properties.image_caption: The Education Building's entrance during the 1919 influenza, masks compulsory
properties.source: NSW State Archives; City of Sydney

properties.name: Parliament House
properties.summary: Australia's oldest parliament still works out of the northern wing of the old colonial hospital that locals called the Rum Hospital. Lawmakers have been meeting behind its Georgian verandah since the 1820s.

timeline.event: The Rum Hospital is finished, three colonnaded Georgian buildings paid for through a monopoly on rum imports. This is its northern wing, the twin of the Mint further down Macquarie Street.
The Legislative Council, created in 1824 as the first legislature in Australia, begins meeting in the surgeon's quarters of the hospital.
The first parliament under responsible government opens here on 22 May, and new chambers slowly grow around the old hospital walls.
A modern block is added at the rear, yet the verandahed Georgian front still serves as the public face of the country's oldest parliament.

stories: Democracy in NSW began as a sub-tenant of a hospital paid for in rum, a fact members of both houses have been reminded of ever since.
With the Mint, Hyde Park Barracks and Sydney Hospital, it completes Macquarie Street's run of buildings older than the institutions inside them.
properties.image_caption: The Rum Hospital's north wing, Australia's oldest parliament
properties.source: Parliament of NSW heritage; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Sydney Tower
properties.original_use: Observation tower atop the Centrepoint shopping complex
properties.summary: Since 1981 the golden turret on its slender spire has been the city's easiest landmark to find. Lost anywhere in the basin, you can pick out the tower and know roughly where the centre is.

timeline.event: Donald Crone unveils the first plans for a tower over the Centrepoint shopping centre, a high perch for looking out over the city that was rising around it.
The tower opens to the public in September at about 305 metres, the tallest structure in Sydney. Height rules tied to the airport flight paths have helped it keep that title ever since.
High in the turret, a 162,000-litre water tank does quiet work as a damper, sloshing back against the push of the wind to steady the spire in a gale.
Skywalk opens, sending visitors in jumpsuits out onto an open platform on the outside of the turret.

stories: Orientation in Sydney is a glance for the golden turret: lost anywhere in the basin, find the Tower and you know where the city is. It appears in the background of half this map's modern photographs.
It is held up by 56 cables; laid end to end, the strands would reach from Sydney to Alice Springs, the favourite fact of every school excursion since 1981.
properties.image_caption: The turret on its spire, every Sydneysider's compass needle
properties.source: Sydney Tower heritage; City of Sydney

properties.name: AMP Building, Sydney Cove
properties.summary: The curved glass slab at Circular Quay was the first Sydney building to break the old height limit, and when it opened in 1962 it was the tallest in Australia. Its rooftop deck gave many Sydneysiders their first ever look at the city from above.

timeline.event: Sydney caps its buildings at 150 feet, about the reach of a fire ladder, and the skyline stays low for the next half century.
AMP lodges plans for a 383-foot tower at the Quay, setting off about a year of wrangling with the government and the City Council before approval finally comes.
The curved tower opens on 23 November at 117 metres, the tallest building in Australia. A rooftop deck draws long queues for the view, as most people had never seen their city from above.
Harry Seidler's Australia Square overtakes it, and the AMP Building is folded into the wall of towers it had begun at the Quay.

stories: On opening, AMP ran public tours to the roof, the queue stretched down Alfred Street for the first aerial view of Sydney most people had ever had. The Tower, Australia Square and every harbour-view office since are its descendants.
Its gentle curve follows the Quay, a politeness to the harbour that few of its taller neighbours inherited.
properties.source: City of Sydney; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The AMP Building, Circular Quay (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

properties.name: Capitol Theatre
properties.summary: Inside an old market hall, the Capitol opens onto a make-believe Mediterranean garden, watched over by a ceiling painted as the night sky. It is one of only two of Sydney's grand picture palaces still standing.

timeline.event: The building goes up as the new Belmore Markets. From 1916 it houses Wirth Brothers' circus and a hippodrome, complete with a ring that could sink into a pool for aquatic acts.
On 7 April it reopens as the Capitol, Sydney's atmospheric theatre, its auditorium dressed as a Florentine garden beneath a ceiling of drifting clouds and southern stars.
The grand theatre slides into use as a rock venue, and demolition is proposed more than once.
A full restoration returns it to the 1928 design, and the starlit ceiling shines again over musicals at Haymarket.

stories: The ceiling's stars are arranged as the actual southern sky, the atmospheric theatre's promise was that you watched the show from an open courtyard in a kinder country.
With the State Theatre, it is one of only two survivors of Sydney's picture-palace age, and the only atmospheric one left.
properties.source: Capitol Theatre heritage; City of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Capitol Theatre, Haymarket (Wikimedia Commons)

properties.name: Blues Point Tower
properties.summary: This is Sydney's most argued-over building, the lone survivor of a grand plan that was never built. It stands at 83 metres on Blues Point and divides opinion to this day.

timeline.event: Harry Seidler draws up a whole scheme for McMahons Point, with towers set in parkland stepping down towards the harbour, after a push to zone the area for industry.
Blues Point Tower opens as the first and only piece of that scheme to be built, while the rest dies in council.
Polls regularly name it both the building Sydneysiders most want demolished and a modernist masterpiece, sometimes in the very same survey.

stories: Seidler defended it to his death: the 'eyesore' was meant to be one of a group of towers set in parkland. Standing alone on Billy Blue's point, it became the argument about what Sydney should be, conducted in concrete.
Its checkerboard facade looks straight down the harbour to the Heads; no apartment building in Sydney has a better view or a worse reputation.
properties.image_caption: Seidler's lone tower on Blues Point
properties.source: Seidler Architectural Foundation; North Sydney Council

properties.name: The Great Synagogue
properties.current_use: Synagogue, the mother congregation of Australian Jewry
properties.summary: Thomas Rowe's sandstone synagogue, consecrated in 1878, serves a Jewish community almost as old as the colony itself. It faces Hyde Park with a confidence that matched its congregation's standing in Victorian Sydney.

timeline.event: At least a dozen Jewish convicts arrive with the First Fleet, and organised Jewish life in Australia begins alongside the colony.
The York Street Synagogue, built in an Egyptian Revival style, draws Sydney's congregation together under one roof.
Rowe's Great Synagogue is consecrated opposite Hyde Park, mixing Byzantine and Gothic lines in golden sandstone, with a cast-iron gallery and a ceiling scattered with stars.
The AM Rosenblum Jewish Museum is founded within the synagogue, telling the story of the congregation and its faith.

stories: Rowe designed the Great Synagogue and Sydney Hospital in the same career, two of Macquarie and Elizabeth Streets' finest faces by one hand.
The congregation descends from the First Fleet's Jewish convicts, among the oldest continuous Jewish communities of the modern diaspora.
properties.image_caption: Thomas Rowe's Great Synagogue on Elizabeth Street
properties.source: The Great Synagogue heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: St Andrew's Cathedral
properties.summary: St Andrew's is Australia's oldest cathedral, begun under Governor Macquarie and finally finished by the architect Edmund Blacket nearly fifty years later. It stands beside Sydney Town Hall on the old burial-ground edge of George Street.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie lays a foundation stone on 31 August, but only a few footings go in before Commissioner Bigge judges the scheme too grand and the work stops.
Bishop Broughton makes a fresh start with a modest design by James Hume, and the walls rise slowly.
Edmund Blacket presents himself to Broughton and reshapes the building into the Perpendicular Gothic church we see today.
Bishop Frederic Barker consecrates the cathedral on St Andrew's Day, 30 November, roughly half a century after the first stone.
A major restoration repairs the west front. The cathedral choir, which began in the 1820s before any permanent building stood here, is reckoned Australia's oldest continuous musical body.

stories: Three false starts and fifty years: the cathedral outlasted Bigge's veto, two architects and the squabbles of a colony to become the building that made Blacket's name, the University quadrangle followed.
Its choir is older than the present building: it traces back to the 1820s, before the cathedral was even begun, and is reckoned Australia's oldest continuous musical institution.
properties.image_caption: Blacket's twin-spired cathedral beside the Town Hall
properties.source: St Andrew's Cathedral heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Pyrmont Bridge
properties.summary: Pyrmont Bridge is one of the world's first electrically driven swing bridges, and probably the oldest one still swinging.

timeline.event: The first Pyrmont Bridge opens in March, a private toll crossing at a penny a head, and 20,000 people walk over it in the first fortnight.

Percy Allan's replacement opens in June. It runs 369 metres, with ironbark spans flanking a 1,000-tonne steel swing span, driven by electricity from the nearby Ultimo Powerhouse and counted among the first of its kind in the world.
Road traffic ends in August when the Western Distributor opens overhead, and the old bridge looks set for demolition.
Saved and reopened, it becomes the pedestrian spine of the new Darling Harbour, with the monorail crossing above until 2013.
On its hundredth birthday it joins the State Heritage Register, recognised as the world's oldest surviving electric swing-span bridge.

stories: The swing span pivots through 83 degrees in under a minute, powered, in 1902, by electricity from the Ultimo Powerhouse before most Sydney homes had a light switch. The powerhouse became the Powerhouse Museum; the bridge still swings.
Percy Allan designed over 550 bridges, but this was the showpiece: when the road traffic left in 1981 the engineers' institution helped lead the campaign that saved it.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The swing span open, monorail above

properties.name: St Patrick's Seminary, Manly
properties.summary: Locals call it the Manly castle, the sandstone seminary that Cardinal Patrick Moran raised on the headland above the harbour. It trained Catholic priests for more than a century, then later stood in as Gatsby's mansion on screen.

timeline.event: Cardinal Moran lays the foundation stone on 19 November, claiming one of the grandest sites in Sydney for a national seminary.
The seminary opens in January as a centre for Australasia, Sheerin and Hennessy's Perpendicular Gothic in golden sandstone, built for about seventy thousand pounds.
The Cardinal Cerretti Memorial Chapel is completed, rounding out the group of buildings on the headland.
At the centenary, the tally reaches 1,714 priests trained here, among them four cardinals and forty-one bishops.
The seminary closes in November and clerical training shifts to Strathfield, and the International College of Management moves in the following May.
Baz Luhrmann's The Great Gatsby uses the building as Gatsby's mansion, with its palms erased in post-production to suit an American setting.

stories: Ferry passengers crossing the heads still get the view Moran designed for: a Gothic silhouette crowning the headland, announcing the church's ambition to the harbour.
For The Great Gatsby the palms out front were dug up, trucked away for the shoot, and replanted afterwards; the interiors were all Fox Studios sets.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register no. 1724; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The seminary rising over Manly, c. 1885

properties.name: Art Gallery of New South Wales
properties.summary: The Art Gallery of New South Wales grew out of Walter Liberty Vernon's sandstone temple to art, a building that was never quite finished. Its facade still carries empty niches and allegorical bronze horsemen, and inside it once hosted the court case over a portrait that gripped the nation.

timeline.event: A public meeting in Sydney founds the New South Wales Academy of Art, the institution that would grow into the gallery.
Vernon's sandstone building begins opening in stages. The Grand Oval Lobby follows in 1902 and the facade in 1909, though his planned dome is never built.
Gilbert Bayes' bronze horsemen, The Offerings of Peace and The Offerings of War, take up their posts out front.
In the Dobell case, the Supreme Court rules that his Archibald-winning portrait of Joshua Smith is a true portrait and not a caricature, and the challenge to the prize fails.
Sydney Modern opens across the gully in December, roughly doubling the campus. The two buildings are later named Naala Badu and Naala Nura.

stories: The facade is permanently unfinished: of Percival Ball's six planned bronze panels of the great art epochs, only four were ever cast, he died before the first was done, and the Gothic and Renaissance niches still sit empty.

The 1944 Dobell case put modern art itself in the witness box: two fellow entrants sued over his Joshua Smith portrait defended by the future Chief Justice Garfield Barwick. Dobell won, and the ordeal wrecked his health and the friendship with his sitter.
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW archives; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Vernon's facade, note the empty niches

properties.name: Royal Botanic Garden
properties.summary: This is Australia's oldest botanic garden, laid out on the shore of Farm Cove where the colony first tried to grow its own food. Its director Joseph Maiden later fixed the founding date at 13 June 1816, the day Governor Macquarie opened the road named for his wife Elizabeth, choosing that date because it was her birthday.

timeline.event: Governor Phillip breaks the first government farm at Farm Cove, the earliest European attempt at agriculture on the continent.
Mrs Macquarie's Road opens on 13 June, a date the Governor picked because it was Elizabeth Macquarie's birthday. It was later adopted as the garden's founding day.
Charles Fraser is appointed the first Colonial Botanist, and serious plant collecting begins.
The Garden Palace burns down, and a swathe of colonial records stored inside is lost with it.
Queen Elizabeth II grants the 'Royal' prefix, five years after she came ashore at Farm Cove, the first reigning monarch to set foot on Australian soil.
The Calyx opens on the spot where the Pyramid Glasshouse of 1970-71 once stood.

stories: The Wishing Tree, a Norfolk Island pine of the Macquarie era, required walking around it three times forwards and three times backwards before wishing. When the original was removed in 1945 its wood became souvenirs sold for returned servicemen; a Wollemi pine now grows on the spot.
By the 2010s a colony of 22,000 grey-headed flying foxes had killed 28 mature trees, they were finally persuaded to leave by recorded industrial noise played at dawn.
properties.source: Royal Botanic Garden Sydney; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Botanic Gardens, 1900

properties.name: Australian Museum
properties.summary: The Australian Museum is the country's oldest, born from a despatch in which Earl Bathurst promised £200 a year for a public museum in the colony. Its most-told story is the curator who was carried out the front door still seated in his chair.

timeline.event: Earl Bathurst's despatch sets aside £200 a year for a public museum, the first in Australia. Its early collection is kept in a room in the Colonial Secretary's offices near Bent Street.
Work begins on Mortimer Lewis's Greek Revival building on the William and College Street corner, the museum's first proper home.
The Long Gallery opens to the public in May. It is the oldest museum gallery in Australia, and about 10,000 people pour in during the first week.
James Barnet's College Street wing opens and roughly triples the exhibition space.
The trustees sack curator Gerard Krefft, and when he refuses to leave, two men carry him out the front door still sitting in his chair.
The museum reopens in November after the $57.5 million Project Discover overhaul, with thousands of square metres of new public space.

stories: The Krefft affair: the Darwin-corresponding curator feuded with his creationist trustees until a stolen gold specimen gave them their excuse. Sacked, he barricaded himself in his museum residence, so a trustee, it is said, hired two prizefighters who carried him out the door still in his chair. Krefft won damages but never returned; the chair is displayed among the museum's 200 Treasures.
The museum's first custodian, William Holmes, was accidentally shot dead collecting bird specimens at Moreton Bay in 1831, barely two years into the job.
properties.source: Australian Museum archives; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The museum soon after opening, early 1860s

properties.name: Sydney Grammar School
properties.summary: Big School, the large room finished in 1835, is the oldest surviving schoolroom in Australia. The University of Sydney held its first classes in it before its own grounds at Camperdown existed.

timeline.event: Sydney College is founded near Hyde Park. Chief Justice Sir Francis Forbes lays the foundation stone of the College Street building on 26 January, to a design by Edward Hallen.
The great schoolroom, later known as Big School, opens. It is now the oldest surviving schoolroom in Australia.
The brand new University of Sydney holds its first classes in the building, years before its Camperdown campus was built.
An Act of Parliament incorporates Sydney Grammar School. It opens in the old College buildings on 3 August 1857.
Ten-year-old Banjo Paterson enrols, travelling in from his grandmother's home across the harbour.

stories: One room cradled a school and a university: the 1835 schoolroom hosted the University of Sydney's inaugural classes in 1852, then became the heart of the Grammar School five years later. Three Prime Ministers, Barton, McMahon and Turnbull, learned here.

The school's prehistory runs through Laurence Halloran, the emancipated convict whose 1825 'Sydney Public Free Grammar School' collapsed under his debts, but planted the idea the 1854 Act made permanent.
properties.source: Sydney Grammar School archives; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Big School, Australia's oldest schoolroom

properties.name: Conservatorium of Music
properties.summary: Francis Greenway built this castellated Gothic pile as stables for a grand Government House that Governor Macquarie never managed to raise. The horses got a castle, the colony got a scandal, and today the building is full of music.

timeline.event: Macquarie lays the foundation stone in December for Greenway's castellated stables, meant to serve a new Government House.

The stables are finished in February at around 9,000 pounds, with stallions housed in the octagonal towers while the Governor still makes do with the leaky 1789 residence. Commissioner Bigge is taking careful notes.
The open courtyard is roofed into an auditorium and the building reopens in April as the NSW State Conservatorium of Music.
The Conservatorium High School moves in, and young musicians have been schooled in the castle ever since.
A 69 million dollar redevelopment follows three years of archaeology. A 1790s cistern and an early bakehouse now lie preserved beneath Verbrugghen Hall.

stories: The colony's horses got a Gothic castle while the Governor lived in a cramped, leaking cottage, the mismatch became a prime exhibit in Bigge's inquiry into Macquarie's 'extravagance'. Even Macquarie winced at the ornamented towers; architect Henry Kitchen told Bigge it was 'an incorrect attempt in the style of the castellated Gothic'. It is the only Greenway Gothic building to survive.

Concerts in Verbrugghen Hall happen above a preserved slice of the early town: the 1998–2001 dig under the floor found colonial Sydney intact, cistern, bakehouse, and a rubbish dump spanning 1840 to 1913.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney; Bigge inquiry records
properties.image_caption: Greenway's castle as the Conservatorium, c. 1930s

properties.name: The Mitchell Library
properties.summary: David Scott Mitchell would only hand over his great collection of Australiana if the colony built something worthy of it. The result has a 1644 map laid into its marble floor and a small bronze cat sitting on the windowsill outside.

timeline.event: Construction begins to Walter Liberty Vernon's design, the building being Mitchell's price for his collection.
The Mitchell Library opens in March, three years after its benefactor's death.
The Dixson Wing is added for Sir William Dixson's complementary collection.
The Melocco Brothers of Annandale finish two years of work on the vestibule floor, a marble rendering of Tasman's 1644 map across 45 slabs of Wombeyan marble with brass ships.
The portico, bronze doors, Shakespeare Room and the great glass-roofed reading room complete the building.

stories: The floor is a map with a novel's backstory: drawn for Tasman's voyages in 1644, owned by a Bonaparte prince in Paris, given to Sydney in 1931, then copied stone by stone by Italian-Australian mosaicists while the original went to a vault upstairs.

Outside on Shakespeare Place, Matthew Flinders strides on his 1925 plinth, and on the window ledge behind him sits a small bronze of Trim, his ship's cat, added in 1996. The library holds the explorer's papers; the cat gets the fan mail.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The reading room soon after opening, 1911–12

properties.name: The Australia Hotel
properties.summary: For eighty years this was the grandest place to stay in Sydney, opened by Sarah Bernhardt and styled 'The Hotel of the Commonwealth'. It was knocked down in the early 1970s to make way for the MLC Centre, and its loss helped stiffen the city's resolve to protect what was left.

timeline.event: Premier Sir Henry Parkes lays the foundation stone on Castlereagh Street, with the Mansfield Brothers as architects and Norman Selfe as engineer.
The hotel opens as 'The Hotel of the Commonwealth', with Sarah Bernhardt doing the honours and signing the register first.
Emil Sodersten's Art Deco extension reaches through to Martin Place, all black Carrara marble, silver-etched glass and a circular black staircase.
One of Australia's first television demonstrations is staged in the hotel ballroom.
The hotel closes on 30 June and the wreckers move in for Harry Seidler's MLC Centre.
The Theatre Royal reopens inside the MLC Centre, the one piece of the old block carried over into the new.

stories: For eighty years it was where the world stayed: Bernhardt at the opening, Marlene Dietrich (rooms 707–708 were named for her), Robert Helpmann in a permanent suite, and one woman who lived there 31 years. But not the Beatles: in 1964 the Australia was among four grand hotels that refused them, so the little Sheraton in Potts Point got the most famous guests of the decade.
Its mahogany staircase climbed from the first floor to the tenth. The BLF's green ban couldn't save the hotel, only the Theatre Royal inside the block, rebuilt as the price of lifting the ban.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Royal Australian Historical Society
properties.image_caption: The Australia soon after opening, c. 1892

properties.name: Powerhouse Museum (Ultimo Power Station)
properties.summary: This power station drove Sydney's electric trams and even swung Pyrmont Bridge open, then was reborn as a museum with a flying boat hung in its old boiler hall.

timeline.event: The museum opens in the Garden Palace, then loses nearly everything in the 1882 fire that destroys the building.
It reopens as the Technological Museum on Harris Street in Ultimo, rebuilt from far humbler beginnings.
Ultimo Power Station fires up as Sydney's first major power station, driving the new electric trams. From 1902 its current also swings Pyrmont Bridge.
The turbines fall silent after 64 years.
The Powerhouse Museum opens in the converted station, and the Catalina Frigate Bird II is hoisted into the boiler-room roof.
Ultimo closes for a 300 million dollar renewal, with reopening planned for 2027, after a proposal to abandon it for Parramatta was reversed. Powerhouse Parramatta opens as a second campus.

stories: The building is its own best exhibit: the boilers here powered the trams from 1899 and opened Pyrmont Bridge from 1902, and Locomotive No. 1, which hauled NSW's first passenger train in 1855, now rests where the coal once burned.

In 1951 the Catalina Frigate Bird II island-hopped from Rose Bay to Chile, the first air crossing of the South Pacific, no rescue cover, crew of five. P.G. Taylor gave her to the museum in 1961, and she has hung in the boiler hall since 1988, claimed as the largest aircraft suspended in any museum.
properties.source: Powerhouse Museum; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The boiler house under construction, 1899

properties.name: Auburn Gallipoli Mosque
properties.summary: Modelled on a mosque in Istanbul and raised by Sydney's largest Turkish community, this classical Ottoman mosque takes its name from the battlefield where two nations first met as enemies and ended up friends.

timeline.event: The first mosque opens nearby in a converted house, its internal walls knocked through to make room to pray.
Work begins on a classical Ottoman mosque, modelled on the Marmara University faculty mosque in Istanbul.
After thirteen years of community-funded building, the mosque opens on 28 November with twin 34-metre minarets, a cascade of domes and room for 1,600 worshippers inside.
Turkish Prime Minister Recep Tayyip Erdogan attends Friday prayers.

stories: The $6 million was raised by the local, largely Turkish community, families who had arrived from the late 1960s and first prayed in a knocked-through Auburn house. The dome rises 22.6 metres over the prayer hall, in deliberate Sinan-tradition proportions.
The name inverts the Anzac story: the former enemy's descendants raised a monument to the friendship that grew out of Gallipoli, in the suburb that holds Australia's largest Turkish community.
properties.source: Cumberland Council heritage study; Auburn Gallipoli Mosque
properties.image_caption: The Gallipoli Mosque, Auburn

properties.name: Camden Park House
properties.summary: This was the seat of the Macarthur wool empire, finished a year after John Macarthur died within sight of it. Seven generations on, his descendants still live here.

timeline.event: Macarthur's 5,000-acre grant in the Cowpastures sets the merino empire going, though it is Elizabeth who keeps the flocks thriving through his long absences.
Work begins on John Verge's Palladian house. By now Macarthur, declared insane, has been moved down to the cottage at Belgenny Farm.
Macarthur dies at Belgenny on 10 April, within sight of his unfinished mansion.
Sons James and William finish the house and move in. William's nursery later sends some 34,000 vine cuttings north to help found South Australia's wine industry.
The estate is listed on the State Heritage Register, probably the oldest post-1788 property in Australia still held by its founding family.

stories: Seven generations of Macarthurs have eaten in the same dining room; the house opens to the public two days a year, the second-last full weekend of September.
The wool king never slept a night in his mansion: carried down to the old Belgenny cottage with his mind gone, he died while the masons were still at work on Verge's wings.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Camden Park House
properties.image_caption: Camden Park House, the Macarthur estate (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.5)

properties.name: Tom Uglys Bridge
properties.summary: This was the bridge that finally ended the holiday punt queues across the Georges River. It is named for a man nobody has ever managed to identify.

timeline.event: A hand-operated punt begins crossing the Georges River at Tom Uglys Point. By the 1920s the holiday queues stretch for hours.
Percy Allan's nine-span truss bridge opens, 499 metres long and the longest in Australia at the time. A deeply resented toll replaces the free punt.
The shire pays off its loan on 31 May and the toll gates come down.
A parallel concrete bridge takes the southbound lanes, leaving the 1929 trusses to carry traffic the other way.

stories: Nobody can prove who Tom Ugly was: a mangled 'Tom Huxley', a one-legged 'Wogul-leg Tom', an Aboriginal man named Tow-weiry, or fisherman Tom Illigley, the rival theories have been argued for over a century, and the bridge wears the mystery on its name plate.
Its designer Percy Allan also built Pyrmont Bridge, Sydney's southern gateway and its harbour swing span come from the same drawing board.
properties.source: NSW heritage; Sutherland Shire histories
properties.image_caption: Tom Uglys Bridge over the Georges River

properties.name: Long Bay Gaol
properties.summary: This prison was built largely by the labour of its own inmates, born of a Federation-era faith that gaols should reform rather than only punish. It later held the city's razor-gang crime queens.

timeline.event: The State Reformatory for Women opens at Malabar to Walter Liberty Vernon's Federation Gothic design, its radial cells fanning out from a glazed timber octagon. It was praised as one of the few purpose-built women's prisons in the world.
The State Penitentiary for Men opens alongside, built largely by prisoner labour and meant for petty offenders rather than hardened criminals.
The women's reformatory population peaks at close to 200.
The gallows begin operating. Executions are carried out here until 1939.
Over the following decades the site is reshaped into today's Long Bay Correctional Complex, still in use.

stories: Its most notorious guests were the razor-gang 'crime queens' Tilly Devine and Kate Leigh, both repeat inmates through the 1920s–30s.

The 1909 women's reformatory was a showpiece of penal reform, a radial plan around an elegant glazed octagon, embodying comptroller W.F. Neitenstein's belief that prisons should reform rather than merely punish.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Long Bay Gaol, Malabar

properties.name: St Patrick's Church Hill
properties.summary: This is the oldest surviving Catholic church in Sydney, raised uphill for the Irish of The Rocks. It was dedicated the day after St Patrick's Day to keep the sectarian peace.

timeline.event: The parish is founded and the foundation stone blessed on 25 August, to serve the largely Irish, ex-convict working class of The Rocks. The land was given by emancipist William Davis, transported for his part in the 1798 Irish uprising.

The church is dedicated on 18 March, chosen as the day after St Patrick's Day to avoid sectarian street violence. William Fernyhough's design was redrawn and built by John Frederick Hilly for the steep Church Hill site.
The French Marist Fathers take over the parish at Father McEncroe's wish, and they run it still.
The church survives the plague-era resumptions that flattened much of The Rocks around it.
It remains one of the busiest Catholic churches in the country, at 20 Grosvenor Street.

stories: It was dedicated on 18 March, not St Patrick's Day itself, precisely to avoid the Catholic–Protestant street violence then feared, a church born practising caution.
Its clustering with the nearby Scots Church and St Philip's Anglican gave the locality its lasting name: Church Hill.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: St Patrick's, photographed in the c.1900 Rocks resumption survey

properties.name: Royal Hospital for Women, Paddington
properties.summary: This Paddington hospital began as an 1820 charity asylum and went on to run the longest continuous antenatal clinic in the British Commonwealth. Much of the eastern suburbs was born here.

timeline.event: The Benevolent Society opens a lying-in asylum in Pitt Street, with a ladies' committee chaired by Elizabeth Macquarie. The first baby arrives that October.
The Pitt Street site is resumed, and the women's hospital shifts to a house called Flinton in Paddington.
The purpose-built Royal Hospital for Women opens on 3 May, to a design by architect George Sydney Jones, with its new wards lit by electricity.
Dr John Cadell Windeyer opens an antenatal clinic on 24 August. It was Australia's pioneering antenatal service and ran without a break for around 69 years.
The hospital moves to Randwick. The Paddington site is turned into apartments arranged around a small commemorative park.

stories: This, not Crown Street, was where antenatal care in Australia began: Windeyer's 1912 clinic ran unbroken for some 69 years.

The charity behind it laid a foundation stone in 1820 for 'this asylum for the poor, blind, aged and infirm', one of Sydney's oldest institutions, which became the place much of the eastern suburbs was born.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Benevolent Society heritage
properties.image_caption: The Royal Hospital for Women, Paddington, 1936

properties.name: Thornton's Scent Bottle (Hyde Park Obelisk)
