properties.summary: This elegant sandstone obelisk on the corner of Hyde Park is really a disguised sewer vent. Sydneysiders named it for the smell.

timeline.event: The obelisk goes up during George Thornton's mayoralty as a ventilation shaft for Sydney's first planned sewerage system, styled in the Egyptian manner of an ancient obelisk.
With the sewage redirected to ocean outfalls, the shaft is put to work venting the city's stormwater drains, a job it still does.
It joins the NSW State Heritage Register on 15 November, a working vent that had passed as civic ornament for almost 150 years.

stories: Sydneysiders soon noticed a smell hanging around the dignified new 'monument' and rechristened it 'Thornton's Scent Bottle', mocking both the mayor and the sewer gas. Releasing it 22 metres up still didn't clear the stink at street level.
It is not a memorial to anyone: behind the Egyptian styling is an educt shaft, built so a working sewer vent could pass as classical ornament on the corner of Hyde Park.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; City of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Hyde Park Obelisk, a sewer vent in classical dress

properties.name: Gladesville Hospital (Tarban Creek Asylum)
properties.summary: Opened in 1838 on the Parramatta River, this sandstone quadrangle was the first asylum in Australia built from scratch for the purpose. It treated the colony's mentally ill for more than 150 years.

timeline.event: The Tarban Creek Lunatic Asylum opens, a sandstone quadrangle of wards around a courtyard designed by Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis. The first patients are admitted in November, women moved from the overcrowded Parramatta Female Factory and the Liverpool Asylum.
It is renamed the Hospital for the Insane, Gladesville. James Barnet later adds further buildings to the grounds.
The institution becomes Gladesville Mental Hospital.
As the psychiatric role winds down, it is folded into Macquarie Hospital.
The last inpatient services close. The heritage sandstone buildings survive and find new uses.

stories: The first patients in 1838 were women moved from the overcrowded Female Factory at Parramatta, the asylum inherited the colony's most desperate cases from buildings never meant to hold them.
Thousands of former patients lie in unmarked graves on and around the site, a legacy of more than 150 years of institutional burial.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Gladesville Hospital, the medical superintendent's residence

properties.name: Her Majesty's Theatre
properties.summary: Sydney has had three theatres named Her Majesty's, and fire claimed the first two of them. Not one of the three still stands.

timeline.event: The first Her Majesty's opens on the corner of Pitt and Market Streets with Henry V. Designed by Morell and Kemp, it was the city's largest and best-equipped theatre.
Fire guts the interior during a run of Ben Hur on 23 March, after the asbestos safety curtain fails to drop.
It reopens on 1 August, rebuilt behind the old facade to a design by William Pitt. The theatre runs until 1933, after which the site is cleared for shops and eventually Westfield.
The Empire Theatre on Quay Street, built in 1927, takes the name Her Majesty's for J.C. Williamson's My Fair Lady. It burns down in 1970.
A third Her Majesty's opens near Central on Quay Street on 30 November. It closes in 2001 and is later replaced by apartments.

stories: The original 1887 theatre opened with Henry V before Governor Lord Carrington, and was grand enough to later feature on Australia's $100 note.

In the 1902 fire the asbestos safety curtain failed to drop, and the interior was gutted early one Sunday after a Ben Hur performance, a grim irony for the theatre built to the colony's first fire-safety code.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; AusStage
properties.image_caption: Her Majesty's ablaze, March 1902

properties.name: Prince Henry Hospital (the Coast Hospital)
properties.summary: Banished to a windswept headland at Little Bay in 1881, the Coast Hospital was where Sydney sent its smallpox, plague and leprosy cases. It later grew into Prince Henry Hospital.

timeline.event: The Coast Hospital opens on a remote 500-acre reserve at Little Bay, set up in a smallpox emergency. It was the first government-controlled public hospital of the post-convict era in NSW.
Nurse training begins on the site.
During Sydney's bubonic plague outbreak the hospital isolates infected patients. Over the years it also treats leprosy, typhoid, influenza and tuberculosis.
The Coast Hospital is renamed Prince Henry Hospital, marking the visit of Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester, though he never set foot in his namesake hospital.
The state government announces the transfer of remaining services to Prince of Wales at Randwick. The site is later redeveloped for housing, with the heritage buildings and cemetery kept.

stories: It was built deliberately remote on a windswept headland because 19th-century medicine held that the infectious had to be geographically banished from the city.
Its cemetery, in use from the 1890s to the 1950s, later became the Dharawal Resting Place, with Aboriginal reburials from 2002.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Coast Hospital, Little Bay

properties.name: Glebe Island Bridge
properties.summary: Percy Allan's electric swing bridge of 1903 carried Victoria Road traffic across Johnstons Bay for most of a century. Today it sits locked open, rusting in the shadow of the Anzac Bridge that replaced it.

timeline.event: Opened on 1 July by Lily See, daughter of Premier John See. The steel Allan-truss swing span over Johnstons Bay was turned by a 600-volt electric motor and could open in about 44 seconds, making it one of the earliest electric swing bridges in Australia.
Closed to traffic on 2 December, the day the Anzac Bridge opened alongside it. The new crossing was first known as the Glebe Island Bridge before it was renamed.
Listed on the NSW State Heritage Register on 29 November, by which point it had stood locked and rusting for nearly two decades.
The government announces plans to restore the bridge and reopen it for pedestrians and cyclists as part of the Bays West renewal.

stories: For most of the 20th century it was a daily commuter artery on the Victoria Road line; since 1995 it has sat permanently swung open, rusting in the shadow of its giant successor.
It and Percy Allan's earlier Pyrmont Bridge pioneered electric swing operation, while the bridges of the day were still worked by hand or steam, two Allan swing bridges, a kilometre apart.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The old Glebe Island Bridge, swung open beside the Anzac Bridge

properties.name: The Wayside Chapel
properties.summary: In 1964 the Reverend Ted Noffs opened the Wayside Chapel in a Kings Cross block of flats, with a dozen chairs and a promise of no fences. It has been an open door for the lost and the down-and-out of the Cross ever since.

timeline.event: Ted Noffs opens the Wayside Chapel on 24 March in an upper room at 29 Hughes Street, Potts Point. It began as a dozen chairs in a block of flats and took its name from a chapel he had preached at in the Black Hills of Dakota.

By now the Wayside has grown into a chapel, coffee shop, drop-in space and crisis centre for the young, the addicted and the marginalised of Kings Cross. Its early premises briefly housed the first office of the Foundation for Aboriginal Affairs.
The Wayside marks its 60th anniversary, still at Hughes Street and still taking in all comers.

stories: Noffs had the front fences torn down and big ground-floor windows put in, so nothing would deter anyone from walking in off the street, a 'no fences' welcome that became the Wayside's creed of 'love over hate'.
The 'Upper Room' coffee shop sat above the sanctuary; Noffs called it a narthex where the sacred met the profane, a refuge for the disaffected of the Cross at the height of the counterculture.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The Wayside Chapel
properties.image_caption: The Wayside Chapel, Hughes Street, Potts Point

properties.name: Iron Cove Bridge
properties.summary: Three bridges in turn have carried Victoria Road across Iron Cove between Drummoyne and Rozelle. The shared path beside the newest one completed the popular Bay Run loop.

timeline.event: The first Iron Cove Bridge opens after about four years of work. Its nine wrought-iron lattice-girder spans gave travellers a road route from Drummoyne towards Ryde without waiting for a ferry.
The worn-out original is replaced by the current four-lane steel-truss bridge, opened on 30 July by Premier J.J. Cahill and designed by Laurie Challis of the Department of Main Roads.

A second parallel bridge opens after strong local protest. It carries a busway and a wide shared path that links into the Bay Run foreshore loop. The old 1882 bridge's sandstone abutments still stand just to the south.

stories: By the 1940s the old bridge's piers had decayed so badly under modern traffic that the whole structure had to be replaced.
The 2011 duplication was fiercely fought by residents and two councils; its consolation prize was the shared path that completed the Bay Run, the popular walking-and-cycling loop around Iron Cove.
properties.source: NSW heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Iron Cove Bridge, on the Bay Run

properties.name: Culwulla Chambers
properties.summary: Culwulla Chambers went up in 1912 as the tallest habitable building in Australia, and the alarm it caused helped lock the whole city's skyline down for decades. Sydney built low until the limit was finally swept aside in the 1950s.

timeline.event: Completed at 67 Castlereagh Street on the corner of King Street, to a design by Spain, Cosh and Minnett. At around 50 metres and 14 storeys, it was the tallest habitable building in the country.

Fears that Sydney would sprout a New York skyline, and that fire ladders could not reach its upper floors, helped drive the Height of Buildings Act, which capped new buildings at 150 feet, about 46 metres.
After the height limit was lifted in the 1950s, the AMP Building at Circular Quay opened and rose past Culwulla, closing out roughly fifty years as Australia's tallest habitable building.

stories: The press hailed it as Sydney's first skyscraper, though it was actually built of load-bearing masonry, not a steel frame, and it cost a record £100,000.
A single office block so unsettled the city that it triggered a law holding the entire skyline down for over four decades, which is why Sydney stayed low until the late 1950s.
properties.source: NSW heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Culwulla Chambers, 1925

properties.name: The AWA Tower
properties.summary: The AWA Tower of 1939 was a radio mast bolted atop an Art Deco office block, and it soared over a city held by law to 150 feet. The trick was that its lattice spire counted as a wireless aerial, not a building.

timeline.event: Completed at 45-47 York Street as the headquarters of Amalgamated Wireless (Australasia). A 14-storey Art Deco block was crowned by a lattice-steel radio mast, reaching about 112 metres overall.
It became the tallest structure in Sydney after the Harbour Bridge. The mast was classed as a wireless aerial, which let the whole thing vault over the 150-foot limit that bound ordinary buildings.
The new AMP Building at Circular Quay, at about 115 metres, finally rose past it.

stories: It was a legal loophole made visible: a city otherwise held to 150 feet, topped by a 112-metre tower because the steel mast was classed as a radio aerial, not occupiable building.
Floodlit above the CBD, the lattice mast, modelled on Berlin's Eiffel-inspired Funkturm, was for decades the glowing symbol of AWA radio, with a viewing platform near the 97-metre level.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW heritage
properties.image_caption: The AWA Tower, 1939

properties.name: The Astor
properties.summary: The Astor opened on Macquarie Street in 1923 as the tallest block of flats in Australia and the country's largest reinforced-concrete structure. Residents did not buy apartments. They bought shares in the company that owned the building, and that is still how the place runs today.

timeline.event: Plans are lodged for the Astor flats at 123 Macquarie Street, designed by Donald Esplin and Stuart Mill Mould for the entrepreneur John O'Brien.
The building opens on 25 October with a rooftop party 13 floors above the street. With 52 flats, it is the tallest residential building in Australia and its largest reinforced-concrete structure.
The National Trust lists the building, which still operates on its original company-title model.

stories: It was a luxury high-rise when nearly all Sydney lived in houses: residents bought shares in The Astor Ltd, £2,300 to £3,250, for the right to a flat, and the building still works that way, often called a living exercise in democracy where every shareholder has an equal vote regardless of flat size.
Not, despite the legend, Australia's first flats, but its tallest of the day, and a pioneering experiment in co-operative apartment living on Macquarie Street.
properties.source: National Trust; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The new Astor flats, Macquarie Street, 1920s

properties.name: The Garrison Church
properties.summary: Holy Trinity at Millers Point is recognised as the first military church built in colonial Australia. The redcoats stationed nearby would march up Argyle Street to worship here, which is how it came to be called the Garrison Church.

timeline.event: Bishop William Broughton lays the foundation stone on 23 June, to a first design by the colonial architect Henry Ginn.
The church is finished in local sandstone. Soldiers from the garrison parade up Argyle Street to its doors, giving the church its lasting name.
Edmund Blacket begins enlarging and enriching the building, work that runs to 1859 and includes the wine-glass shaped pulpit.
Regimental colours and military insignia are dedicated in the nave, and the church is formally renamed Holy Trinity Garrison Church.

stories: It is recognised as the first military garrison church built in colonial Australia: British troops from the Dawes Point Battery marched up Argyle Street to its doors, and its walls still hold their regimental colours and memorials.
A future Prime Minister sat here as a schoolboy, Edmund Barton is said to have had part of his early schooling in the church's hall.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Garrison Church, Millers Point

properties.name: Juniper Hall
properties.summary: Juniper Hall is one of the oldest buildings in Paddington, a grand house raised in the 1820s by the emancipist gin distiller Robert Cooper. He named it for the juniper berry that flavoured the gin and made his money, and reputedly to make good on a promise that his wife would have the finest house in the colony.

timeline.event: Robert Cooper, transported in 1813 for receiving stolen goods and later pardoned, begins building the house, which is finished around 1825 or 1826.
Cooper names it Juniper Hall, after the juniper berries that flavoured his gin, and fills its rooms with his enormous family.
The villa is subdivided and gradually swallowed up behind Oxford Street shopfronts for most of the 20th century.
Saved from demolition, the house is restored by the National Trust with Clive Lucas Stapleton and Partners, and the shops in front of it are cleared away.

stories: A convict transported for receiving stolen goods made a fortune in gin, then named his Paddington mansion after the very juniper berry that flavoured it, reputedly to keep a promise to give his wife the finest house in Sydney.
Cooper fathered a famously huge family, said to be 28 or more children, to fill its grand rooms; for decades the elegant villa lay hidden behind Oxford Street shops before the Trust peeled them away.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; National Trust
properties.image_caption: Juniper Hall, Paddington

properties.name: Rouse Hill House & Farm
properties.summary: Rouse Hill House is a rare colonial time capsule, a Georgian homestead in Sydney's north-west where one family stayed for about six generations and almost nothing was thrown away. Every layer of their life still survives in the rooms.

timeline.event: Richard Rouse, Superintendent of Public Works and Convicts at Parramatta, starts clearing the land and raising his Georgian house on a prominent ridge.
The house is substantially finished, and the family move out from Parramatta over the following years.
By the mid-1820s the Rouse family are settled in, beginning close to 170 years of occupation across six generations of the Rouse and Terry families.
The NSW Government moves to resume the estate. The house finally opens as a 'conserved as found' museum in 1999, with family members lingering on the property in the meantime.

stories: Because no generation ever cleared out the one before, the house is a layered time capsule, faded wallpapers, worn carpets, old letters and everyday things left almost where the family last set them down; it's conserved as found, not restored to a single period.
Richard Rouse's garden and orchard may be the oldest surviving colonial garden in Australia, and the estate keeps its stables, barn and woolshed around the house.
properties.source: Museums of History NSW; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Rouse family at Rouse Hill House, 1859

properties.name: Lennox Bridge, Parramatta
properties.summary: This single sandstone arch carries Church Street over the Parramatta River, cut by convict labour for David Lennox, the stonemason who built the colony's first great bridges.

timeline.event: Scottish stonemason David Lennox arrives in Sydney. He had worked under Thomas Telford on the Menai suspension bridge in Wales, and within weeks of landing he is appointed the colony's superintendent of bridges.
He completes the small horseshoe arch at Glenbrook in the Blue Mountains, usually named as the oldest surviving stone-arch bridge on the Australian mainland.
His single-span arch over the Parramatta River is finished, its sandstone quarried at the nearby Female Factory. The council attaches Lennox's name to it years later.

stories: The 23-metre arch was cut from sandstone quarried at the nearby Female Factory, the bridge literally built from the colony's convict-women's institution.
Lennox went on to raise dozens more bridges, some 53 of them in Victoria after he moved to Melbourne in 1844, and died at Parramatta in 1873.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Lennox Bridge over the Parramatta River

properties.name: The Marble Bar
properties.summary: George Adams called it the finest ale room in the colonies, a Victorian jewel of marble and stained glass that was later taken apart slab by slab and rebuilt inside a 1970s hotel.

timeline.event: The bar opens inside George Adams's Tattersall's Hotel on Pitt Street. Varney Parkes, son of Sir Henry, designs a room of marble, mirrors, stained glass and a domed ceiling, hung with paintings by Julian Ashton, at a reported cost of around 32,000 pounds.
The Victorian hotel closes for demolition to make way for the Sydney Hilton. The whole bar is carefully dismantled, with every slab and mirror numbered for the move.
The bar is reassembled intact in the basement of the new Hilton on George Street, where it still pours today.

stories: The lottery magnate George Adams set out to build 'the finest ale room in the colonies', and Julian Ashton's nudes against marble, bronze and stained glass gave it an opulence rare in a colonial pub.

When the wreckers came, the whole room was taken apart piece by numbered piece and rebuilt beneath a modern international hotel, a Victorian interior preserved inside 1970s concrete. (The same Hilton was the site of the 1978 bombing.)
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW heritage
properties.image_caption: The Marble Bar, now within the Hilton

properties.name: The Toaster (East Circular Quay)
properties.summary: This is the bulky apartment block beside the Opera House that Sydney fought hard, and ultimately failed, to stop.

timeline.event: After an outcry over an even bigger scheme, mocked as a wingless 747 about to hit the Opera House, Prime Minister Paul Keating brokers a deal. The towers come down in height and gain a public colonnade, in return for permission to extend further towards the foreshore.
The Anti-Wall Committee forms, and a Save East Circular Quay campaign follows in 1997. Both campaigns fail to stop the development.
The Bennelong Apartments, designed by Andrew Andersons and PTW, are completed. They are soon nicknamed the Toaster for the shape of No. 1 Macquarie Street.

stories: Sydney's great 1990s development fight: the city campaigned hard against the bulk looming beside the Opera House and blocking the harbour, and lost the towers, but won the ground-level Opera Quays colonnade and boardwalk as the trade-off.
The nickname started with one block, No. 1 Macquarie Street, and spread to the whole complex, Sydney's way of cutting an unloved building down to a kitchen appliance.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney
properties.image_caption: 'The Toaster', East Circular Quay

properties.name: The Grace Building
properties.summary: A Gothic deco skyscraper built for Grace Bros on York Street in 1930, it later served as General Douglas MacArthur's wartime headquarters in Sydney.

timeline.event: The building opens on York Street for Grace Bros, an eleven-storey commercial palace in commercial Gothic and Art Deco by Morrow and Gordon. Its soaring corner tower openly echoes Chicago's Tribune Tower. The lower floors traded as a department store, with offices above.
The Commonwealth requisitions the building under national security regulations as a headquarters for General Douglas MacArthur, supreme commander of Allied forces in the south-west Pacific.
The Commonwealth compulsorily acquires the building, which then served as government offices for decades.
The building is added to the NSW State Heritage Register. It had already reopened in 1997, restored, as the Grace Hotel.

stories: Built for Grace Bros and modelled on the Chicago Tribune Tower, it was one of Sydney's grandest interwar skyscrapers.
Taken over for MacArthur's headquarters in 1943, it spent the war as a nerve-centre of the Pacific campaign before becoming government offices, then a hotel.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Grace Building on the corner of York and King Streets

properties.name: The Lands Department Building
properties.summary: One of James Barnet's grandest buildings, this Bridge Street sandstone palace is ringed with statue niches, many of them still empty.

timeline.event: Construction begins on Bridge Street to Colonial Architect James Barnet's design. The first stage is completed in 1881.

The second stage is built between 1888 and 1892, completed under Colonial Architect Walter Vernon after Barnet's retirement. The whole is faced in Pyrmont sandstone and topped with a copper dome and tower.
A new Australian-made electric clock, built by Prouds Limited and run by a pendulum master clock, is installed in the existing tower.

A statue of colonial surveyor James Meehan is placed in a corner niche, but around half of the 48 niches remain empty. Only 23 statues were ever carved, and many of Barnet's nominees were rejected as mere hunters or excursionists.

stories: The administrative heart of the colony's land dealings, designed to overawe, three storeys of Doric, Ionic and Corinthian orders in honey-coloured sandstone.

Its 48 niches were meant to hold statues of the explorers, statesmen and 'developers' of New South Wales; only about two dozen were ever carved, so the building wears its gaps like a quiz of who history forgot to honour.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Department of Lands Building on Bridge Street

properties.name: Mark Foy's
properties.summary: Mark Foy's Piazza Store of 1909 was a tiled Edwardian palace of shopping, and today the same building houses the Downing Centre courts.

timeline.event: Mark Foy's Piazza Store opens near the corner of Liverpool and Elizabeth Streets, a tiled emporium by McCredie and Anderson part-modelled on the Bon Marche in Paris and credited with Australia's first escalator.
An underground subway links the store to the new Museum railway station, giving shoppers a basement railway entrance.
Mark Foy's goes into receivership and closes. The store trades on briefly as Grace Bros Piazza until 1982.
The heritage building reopens as the Downing Centre, home to Sydney's busy local and district courts.

stories: Founded by the Foy family, who had begun in Oxford Street in 1885, the Piazza Store was a temple of mail-order retail with a ballroom, marble and a Pompeian-tiled facade.
When the shopping ended the building found a second life dispensing justice rather than drapery, the grand halls now corridors of the court.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The former Mark Foy's Piazza Store, now the Downing Centre

properties.name: Nutcote, May Gibbs's House
properties.original_use: Home and studio of author-illustrator May Gibbs
properties.summary: Nutcote is the Neutral Bay home of May Gibbs, the author and illustrator who gave Australia the gumnut babies Snugglepot and Cuddlepie. The little harbourside villa, designed by architect Bertrand James Waterhouse, was where she lived and worked for more than forty years.

timeline.event: May Gibbs and her husband move into the new Spanish Mission bungalow, designed by Bertrand James Waterhouse, who suggested she name it Nutcote.
Gibbs draws her long-running newspaper strips 'Bib and Bub' and 'Tiny Tots' in her studio above the harbour.
May Gibbs dies at Nutcote after 44 years in the house, leaving her copyrights to two children's charities.
After a long public campaign saves it from demolition, a community trust buys and restores the house.
Nutcote opens to the public as a house museum and garden devoted to Gibbs's life and work.

stories: Gibbs's Snugglepot and Cuddlepie, first published in 1918, turned gumnuts, blossom and the Australian bush into a children's mythology, with the menacing Banksia Men as its villains.
She left the royalties from her gumnut-baby books to the Spastic Centre of NSW (now Cerebral Palsy Alliance) and the (Royal) NSW Institute for Deaf and Blind Children.
The harbourside garden was restored to suit the native plants and birds that fed Gibbs's imagination.
properties.image_caption: May Gibbs's studio at Nutcote, Neutral Bay
properties.source: Nutcote Trust; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.name: Hambledon Cottage
properties.original_use: Cottage on the Elizabeth Farm estate
properties.summary: Hambledon Cottage is a small Georgian house finished in 1824 on John Macarthur's Elizabeth Farm estate at Parramatta. It was built as spare accommodation, and over the years it sheltered family, guests and friends of the wealthy wool pioneers.

timeline.event: The cottage is finished as extra accommodation on the Macarthur family's Elizabeth Farm estate. Their son Edward, home from England, is the first to stay in it.
Having passed through the household of guests and tenants over the decades, the cottage moves through a succession of private owners across the late nineteenth century.
The Parramatta and District Historical Society takes the cottage in hand and begins restoring its rooms in colonial style.
Hambledon Cottage marks two hundred years as one of Parramatta's oldest surviving homes.

stories: The cottage is named after Hambledon in Hampshire, England.
Penelope Lucas had come out to the colony with Elizabeth Macarthur's household in 1805 and lived her later years in its rooms.
properties.image_caption: Hambledon Cottage, Parramatta
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Parramatta and District Historical Society

properties.name: Darlinghurst Court House
properties.summary: This Greek Revival courthouse on Taylor Square heard many of Sydney's most notorious criminal trials. It was the first purpose-built courthouse in New South Wales and set the pattern for colonial court design for decades.

timeline.event: Work begins on the central block, designed by Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis with a fluted Doric portico facing Forbes Street. Governor Bourke had pushed for it after worrying about prisoners marched through the streets to the old courts.
The Court of General and Quarter Sessions begins sitting on the site, though the central block is not finished until 1844.
James Barnet adds the flanking courtroom pavilions as the court's workload grows, part of additions made through the 1880s.
The adjoining Darlinghurst Gaol closes. Its cells later become the National Art School, while the court keeps sitting next door.

stories: Defendants were brought up to the dock through an underground passage directly from the gaol next door.
Generations of notorious trials, from bushrangers to the razor-gang era, were heard beneath its Greek Revival pediment.
properties.image_caption: Darlinghurst Court House, Taylor Square
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Lancer Barracks
properties.summary: This is the oldest surviving military barracks still in use on mainland Australia, built under Governor Macquarie. Soldiers have garrisoned the Smith Street site for more than two centuries.

timeline.event: Lieutenant John Watts of the 46th Regiment is commissioned to design Parramatta's first substantial military barracks. Watts also worked on Old Government House and the towers of St John's, Parramatta.
The barracks are completed after the main buildings go up through 1819. Troops have garrisoned the site without a break ever since, which is the basis for its claim as the oldest in the country.
The Parramatta troop of the New South Wales Lancers is raised, and the mounted regiment soon gives the barracks the name it still carries.
The regiment gains its 'Royal' title. Today the unit is the 1st/15th Royal New South Wales Lancers.

stories: The historic Linden House on the grounds holds the Royal NSW Lancers' Memorial Museum, with cavalry relics and a collection of armoured vehicles.
Generations of citizen-soldiers have mustered on its parade ground for more than two centuries.
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Royal NSW Lancers
properties.image_caption: Lancer Barracks, Smith Street, Parramatta

properties.name: Minerva Theatre
properties.architect: C. Bruce Dellit with Crick & Furse
properties.summary: This streamlined Art Deco theatre stands on Orwell Street in Kings Cross and later spent years as the Metro cinema. Its curved lines and porthole windows make it one of Sydney's finest pieces of interwar Moderne design.

timeline.event: The theatre opens on 18 May with the play 'Idiot's Delight'. The Streamline Moderne design came chiefly from cinema specialists Crick and Furse, after an earlier scheme by C. Bruce Dellit was set aside, with interiors by Dudley Ward.
Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer buys the building and converts it to a cinema, renaming it the Metro. Kings Cross knew it by that name for the next two decades.
After a return to live theatre in the 1970s, the building becomes film and television studios, for a time home to George Miller's Kennedy Miller productions.
Gretel Packer's company Sacred Firebird buys the theatre for about 26 million dollars, rescuing it from redevelopment, though its future as a working theatre remains uncertain.

stories: A grand sculptured facade by Rayner Hoff and a Norman Lindsay mural were proposed for the theatre but never carried out.
Its sleek curved lines and porthole windows made it one of Sydney's purest pieces of interwar Moderne design.
properties.source: NSW heritage records; Theatre Heritage Australia
properties.image_caption: The Minerva Theatre, Kings Cross, 1947

properties.name: State Library of New South Wales
properties.summary: This is the oldest library in Australia, grown from an 1826 members' subscription library into the state's great research collection. It holds the nation's richest store of Australiana.

timeline.event: A group of well-to-do Sydney gentlemen meets at the Sydney Hotel to found the Australian Subscription Library and Reading Room. The fees kept it exclusive, and women were not allowed to join.
Deep in debt, the subscription library sells its collection of some 16,000 volumes to the NSW Government, which reopens it as the Free Public Library, the colony's first truly public library.
The library is renamed the Public Library of New South Wales as its collections and reading rooms grow.
The grand sandstone Mitchell wing opens, four years in the building, to house David Scott Mitchell's bequest of Australiana.
The library takes the name it carries today, the State Library of New South Wales.
The modern Macquarie Street wing opens in the Bicentenary year, dedicated by Queen Elizabeth II.

stories: It holds the nation's richest trove of Australiana, among it First Fleet journals, explorers' diaries and the records of colonial life.
The institution traces an unbroken line from a small subscription library of 1826 to a research library serving millions today; in 2026 it marks 200 years.
properties.image_caption: The Free Public Library on Macquarie Street, forerunner of the State Library
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Kirribilli House
properties.summary: This twin-gabled Gothic Revival villa sits at the tip of Kirribilli Point and serves as the Prime Minister's Sydney residence. Its merchant builder is said to have designed the picturesque house himself.

timeline.event: Merchant Adolphus Feez buys a one-acre block at Kirribilli Point for 200 pounds, carved from the neighbouring Wotonga estate that is now Admiralty House.
Feez completes his twin-gabled Gothic Revival villa on the point, known in its early years as Sophienberg, with steep roofs, fretwork and bay windows.
The house is set aside as the official Sydney residence of the Australian Prime Minister, while The Lodge in Canberra stays the primary one.
The house, garden and grounds are added to the Commonwealth Heritage List.

stories: It stands right beside Admiralty House, the Governor-General's Sydney residence, the two grand houses share the tip of Kirribilli Point.
No architect is credited: Feez is said to have designed the picturesque cottage himself.
properties.image_caption: Kirribilli House on its harbour point
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Commonwealth Heritage List

properties.name: The Enmore Theatre
properties.summary: One of Sydney's oldest surviving theatres, this Newtown landmark has been drawing crowds since the silent-film era. The Szarka brothers built it as a picture house, and Hoyts later gave it the Art Deco face it wears today.

timeline.event: Built on Enmore Road by the Szarka brothers, a well-known theatre family, and opened to the public as a silent picture theatre by 1912, with a concert orchestra playing along to the films.
Now owned by Hoyts, the theatre is rebuilt in Art Deco style by architect Charles Bohringer, which is largely the look it keeps today.
It closes as a cinema and reopens in 1969 for a time as a Greek-film house.
It reopens under its old name for live performance and grows into one of Sydney's great music rooms.

stories: It is one of only a handful of surviving Art Deco theatres in Sydney, and among the city's longest-running entertainment venues.
From vaudeville and silent films to rock, comedy and dance parties, the Enmore has reinvented itself for over a century.
properties.image_caption: The Enmore Theatre, Enmore Road
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Trust (NSW)

properties.name: Tempe House
properties.summary: Tempe House is an elegant villa that the architect John Verge designed for the merchant Alexander Brodie Spark in 1834, set on the Cooks River. Spark, who had done the gentleman's tour of Europe, named the estate after the Vale of Tempe in Greece.

timeline.event: The Scottish merchant Alexander Brodie Spark buys land on the Cooks River and names his estate Tempe after the famous valley in Greece.
John Verge designs a refined Greek Revival villa for Spark, and it is built over the next two years.

The estate has passed to the Sisters of the Good Samaritan, who run it as a refuge for women. Cardinal Moran lays the foundation stone of St Magdalen's Retreat, and the grand St Magdalen's Chapel rises beside the house in 1888.
Tempe House and the chapel are listed on the NSW State Heritage Register, and the house is restored as the Wolli Creek apartment towers grow up around it.

stories: Spark called the knoll behind the house 'Mount Olympus', classical conceits for a gentleman's riverside retreat.
It is one of the finest surviving works of John Verge, the architect of Elizabeth Bay House.
properties.image_caption: Tempe House on the Cooks River
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Mamre House
properties.summary: Mamre is the Colonial Georgian farmhouse at the heart of Samuel Marsden's western Sydney estate, a working homestead built on the flats beside South Creek. Marsden was a colonial chaplain and magistrate as well as a farmer, and he ran the property as a model farm of orchards, crops and fine-wool sheep.

timeline.event: Marsden buys 38 acres on South Creek from a lapsed grantee and starts trialling wool production there.
He receives a 1,030-acre Crown grant, building the estate into a substantial sheep and cropping farm.
Work begins on the brick homestead that still stands, raised in stages over the following decade.
The NSW Planning and Environment Commission buys the property, putting its future in public hands.

stories: Marsden was among the colonists experimenting with fine-wool sheep here in the early 1800s, in the years Australian wool was finding its feet.
Set on Orphan School Creek at Orchard Hills, the homestead is one of the oldest surviving in western Sydney.
properties.image_caption: Mamre House, Orchard Hills
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Hayden Orpheum Picture Palace
properties.summary: The Hayden Orpheum is an Art Deco picture palace on Military Road, Cremorne, opened in 1935 and restored half a century later. It still shows films today, complete with a pipe organ that rises before sessions in the grand main auditorium.

timeline.event: The cinema opens on 3 October as the Cremorne Orpheum, a lavish Art Deco picture palace designed by George Newton Kenworthy for the Virgona family.
As the suburban cinema trade declines, the National Trust recognises the building for its heritage value.

After a $2.5 million restoration backed by Mike Walsh's Hayden group, the renamed Hayden Orpheum reopens on 9 December, fitted with a 1925 Wurlitzer organ brought from a theatre in Fresno, California.
Two further screens are added, bringing the cinema to six. It still runs as a much-loved picture house, organ and all.

stories: Its Wurlitzer pipe organ rises from the floor to play before sessions in the grand main auditorium, a survival of the silent-film era.
It is one of the last of Sydney's atmospheric Art Deco suburban picture palaces still operating as a cinema.
properties.image_caption: The Hayden Orpheum, Military Road, Cremorne
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; National Trust (NSW)

properties.name: Brislington
properties.summary: Brislington is the oldest house still standing in the town of Parramatta, a sturdy Georgian brick home finished around 1821. It began as a publican's house, served as a doctor's surgery for nearly a century, and is now a small medical and nursing museum.

timeline.event: The brick house is completed for emancipist trader and publican John Hodge, who reputedly had his convict bricklayers set a diamond pattern into the back wall to mark a lucky card win at a nearby inn.
Dr Walter Brown takes the house, names it Brislington after the Bristol suburb where he was born in 1821, and opens a medical practice.
The Brown family's run of doctors finally ends after about 95 years and three generations, by which time the house had already passed to Parramatta Hospital as a nurses' home.
Brislington reopens as a medical and nursing museum, telling the story of health care in Parramatta.

stories: Three generations of one family of doctors practised here from 1857 into the 1950s.
It is listed on the State Heritage Register as a rare survivor of Georgian Parramatta.
properties.image_caption: Brislington, George Street, Parramatta
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: The Roxy Theatre, Parramatta
properties.summary: The Roxy is a lavish Interwar Spanish Mission picture palace on George Street, the grandest cinema western Sydney had seen when it opened in 1930. Behind its arched entrance and ornate tower sat close to two thousand seats and a Christie organ reputed to be one of the finest in the state.

timeline.event: Opens on George Street with about 1,923 seats and a mighty Christie organ, showing Maurice Chevalier in 'Innocents of Paris'.
Hoyts carves the single auditorium into three cinemas as the age of the one big screen fades.
The Roxy closes as a cinema in March, after 72 years of films.
After a run as a hotel and entertainment venue from 2004, the heritage-listed theatre closes again and waits for a long-hoped-for restoration.

stories: Its richly decorated Spanish Mission interior made it the most glamorous cinema in western Sydney.
It opened with Maurice Chevalier in 'Innocents of Paris' before crowds of Depression-era Parramatta.
properties.image_caption: The Roxy, Parramatta, in the 1930s
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Belgenny Farm
properties.summary: Belgenny Farm was the working home farm of the Macarthurs' Camden Park estate, and it holds the oldest surviving group of colonial farm buildings in Australia. The cluster of barns, stables and an early cottage shows how a great colonial property actually ran.

timeline.event: The Macarthurs receive their first grant of Cowpastures land, and the home farm grows into the working heart of Camden Park.
A cottage, stables and outbuildings go up over the following years, now reckoned the oldest surviving set of colonial farm buildings in the country.
The Macarthurs establish a commercial dairy here, and Belgenny becomes a centre of their pioneering pastoral and stud work.
Belgenny is listed with Camden Park on the NSW State Heritage Register, and it now runs as an agricultural heritage centre.

stories: The Macarthurs' fine-wool experiments, begun at Elizabeth Farm, and their later breeding made this estate central to Australia's wool and dairy industries.
The cluster of barns, stables and the early cottage is a rare survival of how a great colonial farm actually worked.
properties.image_caption: Belgenny Farm, the Macarthurs' home farm at Camden
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Belgenny Farm Trust

properties.name: Collingwood House
properties.summary: Collingwood House at Liverpool was built for Eber Bunker, the American-born sea captain remembered as the father of Australian whaling. It is one of the oldest surviving houses in the district.

timeline.event: The earliest section is built for whaling captain Eber Bunker, a simple brick Georgian house raised under Macquarie's new building rules.
Bunker dies after a quarter of a century at the house on the Georges River flats.
Architect William Weaver carries out major additions for later owners, including a second storey and verandahs.
The house is listed on the NSW State Heritage Register, and it survives as a house museum, one of Liverpool's oldest buildings.

stories: Eber Bunker captained the first whaling voyage out of Sydney in 1791 and is remembered as the father of the Australian whaling and sealing industries.
It is counted among the oldest surviving colonial houses in the Sydney region.
properties.image_caption: Collingwood House, Liverpool
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: St Matthew's Church, Windsor
properties.summary: St Matthew's at Windsor is the convict architect Francis Greenway's hilltop Georgian church, and it counts among the finest buildings of colonial Australia. It is the oldest Anglican church still standing in the country.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie lays the foundation stone for a church designed by Francis Greenway.
The first attempt, condemned by Greenway for poor materials and bad workmanship, is pulled down and started again.
The brick Georgian church stands structurally complete on its hill above the town.
Rev. Samuel Marsden consecrates the church in December, by which time the churchyard already holds graves older than the building itself.

stories: Its serene Georgian proportions make it one of the masterpieces of colonial Australian architecture.
The surrounding cemetery, its oldest stone dating to 1810, is among the oldest European burial grounds in the country.
properties.image_caption: St Matthew's Church, Windsor
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Macquarie Arms Hotel
properties.summary: The Macquarie Arms is an 1815 Georgian inn on Thompson Square at Windsor, widely called the oldest purpose-built pub on the Australian mainland. Governor Macquarie granted the land on the condition that a fine inn be raised on it.

timeline.event: The emancipist Richard Fitzgerald completes the two-storey brick inn, built to Macquarie's specifications for his model town of Windsor.
By the late 1830s the building serves army officers stationed at Windsor and becomes known as the Mess House rather than a public inn.
The Bushell family take it on and run it as a hotel through to the turn of the century.
It is listed on the NSW State Heritage Register as a rare and intact Macquarie-era commercial building.

stories: It is widely called the oldest pub on the Australian mainland, though, as its use lapsed at times, the title of 'oldest continuously licensed' is claimed by rivals.
Built on Macquarie's orders, it was meant to give his model town of Windsor a respectable inn.
properties.image_caption: The Macquarie Arms Hotel, Windsor
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Carss Cottage
properties.current_use: Local history museum in Carss Bush Park
properties.summary: Carss Cottage is a sturdy sandstone house on the high point of Carss Bush Park, looking over Kogarah Bay in Sydney's south. It was built for the Scottish-born cabinetmaker William Carss, and it is reckoned the oldest building in the Kogarah area.

timeline.event: William Carss, a cabinetmaker who had come out from Scotland in 1831, buys the surrounding estate of about 48 hectares for 540 pounds.
The sandstone cottage is built, reputedly by Scottish masons who had worked on Blacket's University of Sydney buildings, with stone quarried on the property.
The cottage opens as a local history museum, leased from the council.
It is added to the NSW State Heritage Register.

properties.image_caption: The front entrance of Carss Cottage, Carss Park
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Kogarah Historical Society

properties.name: Lydham Hall
properties.original_use: Home of master butcher Joseph Davis
properties.current_use: House museum run by the St George Historical Society
properties.summary: Lydham Hall is a sandstone house set on the highest point of the old Bexley estate, with the address now falling in Rockdale. The master butcher Joseph Davis bought the land in 1859 and had Swedish stonemasons, the Benson brothers, build the house, with documentary evidence pointing to the 1870s rather than the long-told date of around 1860. The novelist Christina Stead spent part of her childhood here, and the house now runs as a museum.

timeline.event: The wealthy master butcher Joseph Davis buys 67 acres on the highest point of the former Bexley estate, with sweeping views toward Botany Bay, using it to fatten cattle for his Arncliffe slaughterhouse.

For many years this was given as the building date, but documentary research now points to the house going up in the 1870s, completed around 1878 to 1879 by the Swedish stonemasons known as the Benson brothers.
Bought by Rockdale Council the year before, the house opens to the public as a museum in February 1971, timed to the municipality's centenary.
Lydham Hall is added to the NSW State Heritage Register, having already carried a permanent conservation order since 1986.

properties.image_caption: Lydham Hall, Rockdale
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; St George Historical Society

properties.name: Mortuary Station
properties.original_use: City terminus for funeral trains to Rookwood Cemetery
properties.current_use: Restored heritage building (renamed Regent Street; used for events)
properties.summary: Mortuary Station is an exuberant Victorian Gothic railway station on Regent Street, just south of Central. Colonial Architect James Barnet designed it in carved Pyrmont sandstone, and it opened in June 1869 as the city terminus for Sydney's funeral trains. From its platform, coffins and mourners boarded special services bound for the vast new cemetery at Rookwood, on a railway built largely for the dead.

timeline.event: The station opens in June as Mortuary, James Barnet's Gothic terminus for the funeral trains that ran out to Rookwood. Sculptors carved angels, cherubs and gargoyles into its sandstone.
The funeral terminus closes and the building is renamed Regent Street, after which it handles consignments of horses and dogs.
The Rookwood cemetery line shuts for good, as motor hearses finish off a service that had been fading since the 1930s.
Premier Neville Wran reopens the station in April after the State Rail Authority restores it.
The station is added to the NSW State Heritage Register.

properties.image_caption: Mortuary Station, Regent Street
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Eveleigh Railway Workshops
properties.current_use: Carriageworks arts venue and South Eveleigh (former Australian Technology Park)
properties.summary: Between Redfern and Alexandria sprawls the old Eveleigh Railway Workshops, built up through the 1880s to service the growing New South Wales rail network. At its peak it employed more than 5,000 people and ranked among the largest industrial complexes in the country. The site was a stronghold of organised labour and helped spark the 1917 Great Strike. Today the Carriage Workshops house the Carriageworks arts centre, and the wider grounds have become the South Eveleigh precinct.

timeline.event: The Locomotive Workshops open, joining the Carriage Workshops that went up across the 1880s.
A new card system for timing workers triggers a walkout here and at Randwick that grows into the Great Strike, one of the largest industrial disputes in Australian history.
After roughly a century of work, the workshops close as diesel and electric trains replace steam.
The site joins the NSW State Heritage Register, and the Carriage Workshops are later reborn as the Carriageworks arts venue.

properties.image_caption: Eveleigh Railway Yards (photo by Sam Hood)
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: St Vincent's Hospital
properties.original_use: Free hospital founded by the Sisters of Charity
properties.summary: St Vincent's is a teaching hospital in Darlinghurst, opened in 1857 by the Sisters of Charity and settled on its present Victoria Street site since 1870. It has long been a centre of Australian medicine. Harry Windsor performed the country's first heart transplant here in 1968, and in the 1980s the hospital became the front line of care for people living with HIV and AIDS.

timeline.event: Five Irish Sisters of Charity open St Vincent's as a free hospital at Tarmons, a grand house in Potts Point.
The hospital moves to its present site on Victoria Street, Darlinghurst.
Harry Windsor leads the team that carries out Australia's first heart transplant. The patient, Richard Pye, lived for 45 days.
Australia's first AIDS cases are diagnosed here, and St Vincent's grows into the nation's leading centre for HIV and AIDS care.
Victor Chang performs the first transplant of his new National Heart Transplant Program, which would go on to save thousands of lives.

properties.image_caption: St Vincent's Hospital, Darlinghurst, early 1900s
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; St Vincent's Health Australia

properties.name: Sydney Observatory
properties.summary: The colony's astronomical observatory was built in 1858 on Observatory Hill above The Rocks. Its copper time ball still drops at 1pm each day, the old signal by which ships in the harbour reset their chronometers, once paired with the firing of a gun across the water.

timeline.event: The observatory is built to a design by Colonial Architect Alexander Dawson, who adapted earlier plans by William Weaver, and takes up its role as the seat of the NSW Government Astronomer.
The time ball first falls on 5 June, at noon, before settling on 1pm later that year. Its drop was matched by a gun fired from Dawes Point, and from 1906 from Fort Denison.
The observatory closes its scientific work and reopens as a museum of astronomy.

properties.image_caption: Sydney Observatory and its time-ball tower
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Sze Yup Temple
properties.original_use: Chinese temple to Kwan Ti for the Sze Yup community
properties.summary: This Chinese temple in Glebe was raised by immigrants from the Sze Yup, or Siyi, counties of Guangdong, the source of many Chinese arrivals in colonial Australia. Its central hall, built in 1898, honours Kwan Ti, the general worshipped for his loyalty and courage. It remains one of the oldest Chinese temples still in use in Sydney, and one of only a handful from before the First World War still active in the country.

timeline.event: The central Kwan Ti hall is built, paid for by the Sze Yup Cantonese community of Sydney.
Eastern and western halls are added, among them a hall to Caishen, the god of wealth.
The temple joins the NSW State Heritage Register, having been protected by a conservation order in 1985.

properties.image_caption: The Sze Yup Kwan Ti Temple, Glebe
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Royal North Shore Hospital
properties.summary: The great public hospital of Sydney's North Shore started small. It opened in 1888 as a 14-bed cottage hospital and grew, on its St Leonards site, into one of the city's largest teaching hospitals.

timeline.event: It opens as the North Shore Cottage Hospital with 14 beds, a year after Sir Henry Parkes laid the foundation stone in 1887.
The hospital moves to its present site at St Leonards, now a 48-bed building bearing the title Royal granted in 1902.
It becomes a teaching hospital of the University of Sydney.

properties.image_caption: Royal North Shore Hospital, St Leonards
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Royal North Shore Hospital

properties.name: Sydney Trades Hall
properties.original_use: Headquarters and meeting place of the trade unions
properties.summary: For well over a century this has been the headquarters of the New South Wales labour movement, standing on the corner of Goulburn and Dixon Streets. The first section went up from 1888, designed by John Smedley, one of the first Australian-born architects, and the building grew in stages over the following decades.

timeline.event: Governor Lord Carrington lays the foundation stone on 28 January, for a building John Smedley designed for the Trades Hall Association.
The building reaches its present form, after staged additions by the firm Spain, Cosh and Minnett that followed Smedley's Victorian Italianate style.
It is added to the NSW State Heritage Register and remains the home of Unions NSW.

properties.image_caption: Sydney Trades Hall, Goulburn Street
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: The Sydney Cenotaph
properties.current_use: War memorial; site of the Anzac Day dawn service
properties.summary: The Sydney Cenotaph is the granite war memorial that anchors the eastern end of Martin Place, guarded by bronze figures of a soldier and a sailor cast from a design by the sculptor Sir Bertram Mackennal. Dedicated in the late 1920s, it is one of central Sydney's oldest First World War memorials and the focus of the city's Anzac Day dawn service.

timeline.event: The Moruya granite cenotaph is dedicated on 8 August before Mackennal's bronze figures have arrived.
With the sentinel soldier and sailor now in place, the finished memorial is unveiled on 21 February by Premier Thomas Bavin, with Sir John Monash giving the address.
The cenotaph is added to the NSW State Heritage Register.

properties.image_caption: The Sydney Cenotaph, Martin Place
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Prince of Wales Hospital
properties.summary: This major teaching hospital at Randwick began in 1858 as the Asylum for Destitute Children, a huge institution on 60 acres at High Cross that housed up to 800 orphaned and abandoned children at a time. After the children left, the grounds served as a military and repatriation hospital, and in 1953 the site took the name Prince of Wales Hospital.

timeline.event: The Asylum for Destitute Children opens on a 60-acre grant at High Cross, Randwick, run by the Society for the Relief of Destitute Children.
The asylum is resumed and reopens as the Fourth Australian military hospital, later a repatriation hospital.
The repatriation hospital is renamed the Prince of Wales Hospital and managed as an annex of Sydney Hospital.
The hospital is reorganised for teaching and training, and it is now affiliated with UNSW Sydney.

properties.image_caption: Prince of Wales Hospital, Randwick
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; South Eastern Sydney LHD

properties.name: Anthony Hordern & Sons
properties.summary: The Palace Emporium on Brickfield Hill was reckoned one of the largest department stores in the world, where Sydney could buy almost anything 'from a needle to an anchor'. The Hordern family traded under the sign of a spreading oak with the motto 'While I Live I'll Grow'.

timeline.event: Anthony Hordern & Sons open a grand emporium on Brickfield Hill, on the block bounded by George, Pitt and Goulburn Streets.
Fire destroys the store on 10 July, and trading shifts almost at once to the Exhibition Building at Prince Alfred Park.
A six-storey New Palace Emporium opens on the site, its main entrance fitted out in imported Italian marble.
Waltons takes over the business in January and the famous store winds down within a few years.
The building is controversially demolished for the World Square project, which then sits as a vacant site until its completion in 2004.

properties.image_caption: Anthony Hordern & Sons' Palace Emporium, Brickfield Hill, 1935
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: North Sydney Oval
properties.summary: North Sydney Oval sits in the south-west corner of St Leonards Park and is one of the oldest sporting grounds in Australia. The first cricket pitch was laid here in December 1867, and the game has been played on the ground ever since. For decades it was home to the North Sydney 'Bears' rugby league club, whose crowds watched from the handsome grandstand built in 1929.

timeline.event: The first cricket pitch is laid on the public reserve at St Leonards Park on 6 December, making this one of the earliest grounds of its kind in the colony.
North Sydney rugby league, formed in 1908, is by now playing its football at the oval, already a busy district sporting venue.

The Duncan Thompson Stand is built and is then the largest suburban grandstand in New South Wales. It carries the name of the North Sydney captain who led the club to a premiership in the early 1920s.
The North Sydney Bears lose their top-grade place after a merger, though grade football and cricket carry on at the ground.

properties.image_caption: Cricket at North Sydney Oval, 1930s
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; North Sydney Council

properties.name: Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children
properties.summary: The first children's hospital in New South Wales opened in 1880 and spent most of its life at Camperdown, where generations of sick children were treated before the hospital moved to Westmead in 1995. It began as the Sydney Hospital for Sick Children at Glebe Point.

timeline.event: The Sydney Hospital for Sick Children opens at Glebe Point on 1 January, the first dedicated children's hospital in the colony.
King Edward VII grants the title 'Royal' and Queen Alexandra lends her name, so it becomes the Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children.
The hospital moves to a large new complex on Pyrmont Bridge Road, Camperdown.
After 89 years at Camperdown the hospital relocates to Westmead, and the old buildings are later converted into apartments.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Children's Hospitals Network
properties.image_caption: The Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children, Camperdown (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

properties.name: Lansdowne Bridge
properties.current_use: Road bridge on the old Hume Highway
properties.summary: This graceful sandstone arch carries traffic over Prospect Creek near Liverpool. Convicts built it to the design of David Lennox, the Scottish stonemason who served as the colony's Superintendent of Bridges, and it opened in 1836. Its single arch is still the largest masonry span of any surviving bridge in Australia, and it is widely regarded as Lennox's masterpiece.

timeline.event: Convict labourers begin work under David Lennox on a single great sandstone arch over Prospect Creek, using stone quarried along the Georges River.
The Lansdowne Bridge opens on 26 January, its 34-metre arch the largest masonry span in the colony.
The bridge is added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 20 June.

properties.image_caption: Lansdowne Bridge over Prospect Creek, Lansvale
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Leichhardt Oval
properties.summary: Leichhardt Oval is a much loved old rugby league ground in the inner west, opened on Anzac Day 1934 after a five thousand pound makeover. For decades it was home to the Balmain Tigers, and fans still treasure its steep grassy hill and the stands that crowd close to the field. Many rate it one of the great suburban grounds of the game.

timeline.event: Leichhardt Oval opens on Anzac Day. Balmain plays the first match here, going down to Western Suburbs 18 to 5.
The ground takes its place as a regular venue for Balmain, and the club shifts all its training and matches there the following year.
Balmain has joined Western Suburbs to form the Wests Tigers, and the oval continues as an NRL home ground for a handful of matches each season.

properties.image_caption: The Hill at Leichhardt Oval
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Inner West Council

properties.name: Theatre Royal
properties.summary: The Theatre Royal carries the oldest name in Australian theatre. A house under that name has played in Sydney since 1833, through a run of different buildings, and the current one in King Street has staged the city's big musicals and dramas for half a century.

timeline.event: Barnett Levey opens the first Theatre Royal off George Street, giving Sydney its first proper purpose-built theatre.
A grand new Theatre Royal opens on Castlereagh Street, designed by architect John Frederick Hilly. It is patched up after an 1892 fire and serves the city for decades.
The present Theatre Royal opens in King Street, designed by Harry Seidler inside the new MLC Centre. Union pressure had forced the developer to fit a replacement theatre into the redevelopment.
The theatre reopens after a major refurbishment, having gone dark in 2016 during work on the building above it.

properties.image_caption: Ballets Russes at the Theatre Royal, Sydney, 1937
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Balmain Watch House
properties.summary: This small Georgian lock-up at 179 Darling Street went up in 1854 and is the oldest surviving police watch-house in Sydney. Constables once held offenders here overnight, in cells set behind the constable's own quarters.

timeline.event: The watch-house is finished on land bought the year before. The design is credited to Colonial Architect Edmund Blacket, with a guard room, a constable's room, and separate male and female cells.
A second storey and two rear cells are added, each with its own privy and exercise yard. The building stays in use as a lock-up and police residence into the 1920s.
Saved from demolition, the watch-house later passes to the National Trust and the Balmain Association, which runs it as a small museum and gallery.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Balmain Association

properties.name: Sydney Benevolent Asylum
properties.original_use: Refuge for the destitute and a lying-in hospital
properties.current_use: Demolished 1901; site now part of Central Station
properties.summary: The Benevolent Asylum was the colony's great refuge for the poor, the old and the homeless, opened in 1821 on the edge of town near where Central Station now stands. It was also a lying-in hospital, and thousands of Sydneysiders drew their first breath inside its walls.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie opens the asylum at the corner of Pitt and Devonshire Streets, with the Benevolent Society running its daily affairs.
Part of the asylum is set aside as a lying-in hospital for poor and deserted mothers, delivering generations of children who had nowhere else to go.
The asylum becomes a teaching hospital for University of Sydney medical students, with a particular focus on obstetric training.
The buildings are pulled down when the site is resumed for Central Station. The Society's work carries on at the new Royal Hospital for Women in Paddington.

properties.image_caption: The Sydney Benevolent Asylum
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The Benevolent Society

properties.name: Ebenezer Church
properties.summary: This little stone church sits on a rise above the Hawkesbury River, raised in 1809 by free settler families. It is the oldest surviving church building still in use anywhere in Australia.

timeline.event: Eight free settler families arrive in Sydney aboard the Coromandel and soon take up land grants at Portland Head on the Hawkesbury.
The settlers complete a stone church on four acres given by Owen Cavanough, a First Fleet sailor. The building, designed by Andrew Johnston, serves as both church and school.
A schoolmaster's residence is finished beside the church. It is regarded as the oldest surviving school building in Australia.
The congregation is formally established as Presbyterian, the first of its kind in the colony.

properties.image_caption: Ebenezer Church, on the Hawkesbury
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Paddington Reservoir
properties.summary: A grand brick-vaulted reservoir was built beneath Oxford Street in the 1860s and 1870s to water Sydney's eastern suburbs. After decades forgotten and ruined, it reopened in 2009 as the award-winning Paddington Reservoir Gardens, with a sunken garden tucked among the old chambers.

timeline.event: The western chamber is completed to engineer Edward Bell's design, holding water for the eastern suburbs. The eastern chamber follows in 1878.
The reservoir is taken out of service. The site is later used for storage and, in time, as a service station.
A long-neglected section of the roof gives way and collapses.
The site reopens as the Paddington Reservoir Gardens, a sunken garden set into the old chambers, going on to win a string of national and international design awards.

properties.image_caption: The Paddington Reservoir Gardens
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; City of Sydney

properties.name: Sydney Jewish Museum
properties.original_use: Maccabean Hall, WWI memorial and community centre
properties.current_use: Museum of the Holocaust and Australian Jewish history
properties.summary: The Maccabean Hall in Darlinghurst was built in 1923 to honour the Jewish men of New South Wales who served in the First World War. In 1992 a group of Holocaust survivors opened a museum inside it to record both the horror they had lived through and the longer story of Jewish life in Australia.

timeline.event: The Maccabean Hall opens on Armistice Day, dedicated by Sir John Monash as a memorial to the Jewish servicemen of New South Wales who fought in the First World War.
The Sydney Jewish Museum opens inside the hall, the work of Holocaust survivors led by John Saunders, who wanted their testimony kept where the public could find it.

properties.image_caption: The Sydney Jewish Museum, Darlinghurst
properties.source: Sydney Jewish Museum; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Cathedral of the Annunciation
properties.summary: This sandstone church on Cleveland Street in Redfern was built in the 1850s as an Anglican parish church to Edmund Blacket's design. Since 1970 it has been the cathedral of the Greek Orthodox Archbishop of Australia and a symbol of Sydney's large postwar Greek community.

timeline.event: The foundation stone is laid on St Paul's Day in January 1848 for St Paul's, an Anglican church designed by Edmund Blacket in a Decorated Gothic style. The building is largely complete by its consecration in August 1855.
With the inner-city Anglican congregation in decline and Sydney's Greek community growing fast, the church is sold to the Greek Orthodox Archdiocese.
Archbishop Ezekiel reconsecrates the church in November as the Cathedral of the Annunciation of Our Lady, the seat of the Greek Orthodox Archbishop of Australia.

properties.image_caption: The Cathedral of the Annunciation, Cleveland Street
properties.source: Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of Australia; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Ritz Cinema
properties.summary: The Ritz is an Art Deco picture palace on St Pauls Street, Randwick, designed by Aaron Bolot and opened in 1937. It has shown films almost without a break ever since, one of the last of Sydney's golden-age cinemas still pulling a crowd.

timeline.event: The Ritz opens on St Pauls Street, a restrained Art Deco cinema designed by Aaron Bolot, a Crimean-born architect who also drew up grand apartment blocks across town.
A demolition plan in the late 1980s spurs the council and the state to act, and a permanent conservation order saves the building in March.
Still open and independently run, with extra screens slotted in around the original Art Deco auditorium to keep pace with the multiplex age.

properties.image_caption: The Ritz Cinema, Randwick
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Willoughby Incinerator
properties.current_use: Art gallery and cafe (Incinerator Art Space)
properties.summary: This municipal rubbish incinerator on the north shore was given an unlikely grandeur by Walter Burley Griffin, the architect of Canberra. Long after the fires went cold, it came back to life as an art gallery and cafe.

timeline.event: The incinerator is completed to a design by Griffin and his partner Eric Nicholls, one of thirteen such buildings the pair created across Australia through the Depression years. It starts burning waste in May and is formally opened that September.
Added to the NSW State Heritage Register, recognised as one of the finest of Griffin's incinerators.
Restored and reopened as the Incinerator Art Space, with a gallery, artist studios and a cafe.

properties.image_caption: The Willoughby Incinerator, designed by Walter Burley Griffin
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: The Concordia Club
properties.summary: The Concordia Club began in 1883 as a group of German card players meeting in a Sydney home, and it grew into the social heart of the city's German-speaking community. It is one of the oldest community clubs in New South Wales and is still going today.

timeline.event: German residents found the Concordia Club, at first just a circle of card players gathered at the home of August and Carl Sommer.
Amid the anti-German feeling of the First World War the club shuts its doors, as German associations across the country did, and revives only once the war is past.
After roughly six moves over the decades, including a long spell of more than fifty years at Stanmore, the club settles into the former Tempe Bowling Club opposite Tempe station, where it remains.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Concordia Club
properties.image_caption: The German Club, 89 Phillip Street, c.1900–1910 (Wikimedia, public domain)

properties.name: North Sydney Olympic Pool
properties.summary: This swimming pool at Milsons Point opened in 1936, tucked right under the Harbour Bridge and beside Luna Park. It earned a fierce reputation for fast water, with 86 world records set there over the years.

timeline.event: The pool opens in April, designed by Rudder and Grout, and is hailed as 'the wonder pool of Australasia', its lanes filled with treated seawater pumped from the harbour.
Hosts the swimming and diving for the British Empire Games, the only such Games staged in its water.
By the late 1970s the baths have long been a record factory, swimmers such as Jon and Ilsa Konrads and Lorraine Crapp among those who set marks here. In all, 86 world records would fall in this pool.
Closes in February for a major redevelopment, with reopening eventually set for August 2026.

properties.image_caption: Dawn Fraser and Ilsa Konrads at North Sydney Olympic Pool, 1960
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; North Sydney Council

properties.name: Museum Railway Station
properties.summary: Museum opened beneath Hyde Park on 20 December 1926, and together with St James it gave Sydney its first underground railway stations. The line was the realisation of John Bradfield's long scheme for an electric city railway.

timeline.event: Engineer John Bradfield lays out his plan for an electrified underground railway looping through the city.
Museum and St James open on 20 December, the first underground stations in Australia, their roundel signs borrowed from the London Underground.
The station joins the NSW State Heritage Register with its 1920s platforms still largely intact.

properties.image_caption: Museum Railway Station
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Georges Head Battery
properties.summary: Georges Head Battery was cut into the rock of the Mosman headland from 1872 to guard the entrance to Sydney Harbour. The colony had to look to its own defence after the British garrison sailed home in 1870.

timeline.event: Barracks go up on Georges Heights to designs by Colonial Architect James Barnet, the year after British troops withdraw from the colony.
Work starts on the casemate battery and its tunnels, meant to fire on enemy ships before they could reach the inner harbour.
The defences are strengthened through the Second World War, including guns set against submarines.
The fortifications are listed on the NSW State Heritage Register, the gun pits and tunnels surviving inside Sydney Harbour National Park.

properties.image_caption: A 6-inch gun at Georges Head, 1892
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Sydney Harbour Federation Trust

properties.name: The Tivoli Theatre
properties.summary: The Tivoli was Sydney's home of variety theatre, the much-loved 'Tiv' on Castlereagh Street. For more than thirty years its vaudeville, revue and music-hall acts played to packed houses.

timeline.event: The Cockney comic Harry Rickards takes over the Garrick Theatre on Castlereagh Street and renames it the Tivoli, the hub of his variety circuit.
Rickards reopens the Tivoli on 12 April, rebuilt in about seven months after fire destroyed it in 1899.
J.C. Williamson's closes the Tiv on 28 September, ending its long run of vaudeville, and the building is soon demolished.
The Art Deco Embassy Cinema opens on the old Tivoli site on 1 June, later cleared in the 1980s for the Centrepoint development.

properties.image_caption: The Tivoli Theatre hall, Castlereagh Street
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Central Synagogue
properties.current_use: Synagogue, heart of eastern-suburbs Jewish Sydney
properties.summary: Central Synagogue at Bondi Junction is the anchor of Jewish Sydney in the eastern suburbs, and it holds the largest Jewish congregation in Australasia. Its community swelled after the Second World War as Holocaust survivors made their homes nearby.

timeline.event: The congregation forms in Surry Hills, founded to carry Eastern European tradition into Sydney and to hold people to their faith.
The congregation settles on its present site in Bon Accord Avenue, Bondi Junction, as the Jewish community gathers in the eastern suburbs after the postwar arrival of Holocaust survivors.
A new synagogue is dedicated in May, rebuilt after an electrical fire gutted the building in September 1994.

properties.image_caption: The Central Synagogue, Bondi Junction
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Central Synagogue

properties.name: Concord Repatriation Hospital
properties.summary: Concord opened on the Parramatta River in 1941 as a wartime hospital for the sick and wounded of the Second World War. Its tall Streamline Moderne tower became a landmark for generations of veterans.

timeline.event: The hospital opens on 11 March as the 113th Australian General Hospital, the Yaralla Military Hospital, on the Walker family's Yaralla estate. By 1942 it held about 2,000 beds and was the largest in the southern hemisphere.
Its Streamline Moderne main building, designed by Stephenson and Turner, wins the Sulman architecture award.
The Repatriation Commission takes over on 19 May, running it as the Repatriation General Hospital, Concord, caring for returned servicemen for decades.

properties.image_caption: Concord Repatriation Hospital and its tower
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Local Health District

properties.name: The Regent Theatre
properties.summary: The Regent was Hoyts' grand picture palace on George Street, opening in 1928 with a vast auditorium, a resident orchestra and a mighty Wurlitzer organ. Its demolition in 1988, after years of public campaigning to save it, became a byword for everything Sydney had let slip away.
