timeline.event: The Regent opens as Hoyts' flagship cinema, designed by the Melbourne architect Cedric Ballantyne and seating close to 2,300 people. A 40-piece orchestra and a Wurlitzer pipe organ filled the room before the films began.
The cinema shows its last film on 26 May and closes its doors.
Despite a long campaign to save it, the Regent is demolished for developer Leon Fink after a court allows its conservation order to be lifted, and the George Street site is cleared for redevelopment.

properties.image_caption: A queue at the Regent Theatre, George Street
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Justice & Police Museum
properties.original_use: Water Police Court and police station
properties.current_use: Museum of crime, policing and justice
properties.summary: This was the Water Police Court at the edge of Circular Quay, where for more than a century the rough business of the waterfront came before the magistrates. Today it is a museum of crime, policing and justice in New South Wales.

timeline.event: The Water Police Court goes up to a design by Colonial Architect Edmund Blacket, a low sandstone building with an arched colonnaded porch. A police station follows next door in 1857.
A second courthouse is added under Colonial Architect James Barnet as the complex takes on the steady flow of waterfront crime.
The buildings open as the Justice and Police Museum under the Historic Houses Trust, telling the story of crime and justice in the colony and the state.

properties.image_caption: The Justice & Police Museum (former Water Police Court)
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Bella Vista Farm
properties.summary: Bella Vista Farm sits on a hilltop in the Hills district, on land first granted in 1799 and later part of John and Elizabeth Macarthur's Seven Hills Farm. The Pearce family bought it in 1842 and turned it into one of the colony's great citrus orchards, and its Victorian homestead is now a public heritage park.

timeline.event: About 980 acres in the Seven Hills district are granted to Joseph Foveaux and Charles Grimes as Stock Farm. John and Elizabeth Macarthur buy the land around 1801, folding it into the holding later called Seven Hills Farm.

William Pearce buys the farm from Isabella Acres. From the 1860s the family builds the surviving homestead and runs one of the colony's largest citrus orchards, with Edward Henry Pearce later called the biggest orange grower in the colony.
Baulkham Hills Shire Council buys the farm and opens it as Bella Vista Farm Park, the name a nod to the beautiful view across the hills.

properties.image_caption: Bella Vista Farm homestead
properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; The Hills Shire Council

properties.name: Addison Road Community Centre
properties.summary: The army took over this patch of Marrickville in 1913 and turned it into a depot that mustered, drilled and farewelled soldiers through both world wars. Since 1976 the old huts and drill halls have housed one of Sydney's busiest gatherings of community, arts and migrant groups.

timeline.event: The Commonwealth buys the land for an army depot. The creek crossing the site is piped underground, and once the First World War breaks out the depot becomes a recruitment and embarkation point for soldiers bound for the Western Front.
By the 1960s the depot is again moving troops, this time National Servicemen, and stands among the inner-west sites bound up with the era of Vietnam conscription.

The army hands over a worn and run-down depot. Volunteers from welfare groups, children's services, artists and environmentalists rebuild the huts, and the place grows into a home for dozens of multicultural and community organisations.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Addison Road Community Organisation

properties.name: St Paul's College
properties.summary: St Paul's College is the oldest university residential college in Australia, founded as the Anglican college of the new University of Sydney in 1856. Edmund Blacket gave it a sandstone home in the Early English Gothic style on the western edge of the campus.

timeline.event: St Paul's is founded as the Anglican college of the new University of Sydney. Governor Sir William Denison lays the foundation stone on 25 January, the feast day of the conversion of St Paul.

The College Hall is completed, a year after Blacket's first range of student rooms and library opened in 1858 and the earliest residents moved in, well before the University's own buildings were ready.

properties.image_caption: St Paul's College, University of Sydney
properties.source: NSW heritage records; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Rose Seidler House
properties.summary: Rose Seidler House at Wahroonga was Harry Seidler's first building in Australia, a crisp flat-roofed modernist box he designed for his parents and finished in 1950. It dropped pure International Style onto the leafy North Shore and is now kept as a museum of mid-century design.

timeline.event: Harry Seidler, recently arrived from Europe by way of the United States, designs a flat-roofed International Style house at Wahroonga for his parents Rose and Max. It is his first commission in Australia, and Rose drives the brief, which is why the house carries her name.
The house is finished, its open plan, entrance ramp, sun deck and free-standing walls a startling sight among the bushland gardens of the North Shore.
It wins the Royal Australian Institute of Architects' Sir John Sulman Medal, the first of five Seidler would collect.
After Seidler handed the house to the State in 1988, it opens to the public as a museum, kept with its original furnishings and murals.

properties.source: Museums of History NSW; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Rose Seidler House, Wahroonga, photographed in 1951

properties.name: Denham Court
properties.summary: Denham Court is a grand colonial estate near Liverpool, its plain early house turned into a Regency homestead by the fashionable architect John Verge in the 1830s. A short walk away stands the little church of St Mary the Virgin, which the Brooks family raised over their own graves.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie grants some 500 acres to Judge-Advocate Richard Atkins, who names the place Denham Court after his English family seat but never settles there.
Captain Richard Brooks, a trader across the Indian and Pacific oceans, takes over the property in part settlement of Atkins's debts, though he does not move out until the mid-1820s.
John Verge reworks the modest house into a grand Regency homestead, adding a two-storey centre and flanking wings with a colonnaded verandah.
St Mary the Virgin, a private chapel Verge designed over the Brooks family vault, is consecrated by Bishop Broughton. It is said to be the oldest former private chapel in Australia still in use.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: St Mary the Virgin (1838), the Verge-designed chapel on the Denham Court estate

properties.name: Convent of the Sacred Heart, Rose Bay
properties.summary: The Society of the Sacred Heart founded this harbourside convent and girls' school at Rose Bay in 1882, the order's first foundation in Australia. The Canadian-born architect John Horbury Hunt gave it both its towering sandstone convent and the Gothic chapel that still crowns the ridge above the bay.

timeline.event: Five nuns of the Society of the Sacred Heart, newly arrived from overseas, take over a house called Claremont at Rose Bay and open a girls' school on 20 July, the order's first foundation in Australia.
Cardinal Moran opens the first permanent building, a five-storey sandstone convent designed by John Horbury Hunt and built from stone quarried on the ridge it overlooks.
Horbury Hunt's Gothic Revival chapel is completed, having been under construction since 1897.
The Rose Bay school joins with Kincoppal at Elizabeth Bay to become Kincoppal-Rose Bay School of the Sacred Heart.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Convent of the Sacred Heart, Rose Bay

properties.name: Strickland House
properties.summary: Strickland House is a grand Italianate mansion on the Vaucluse foreshore, built in the 1850s as 'Carrara' for John Hosking, Sydney's first popularly elected mayor. It later served as a women's convalescent home and is now a public estate above the harbour.

timeline.event: John Hosking, who became Sydney's first popularly elected mayor in 1842, completes the Italianate villa 'Carrara'. John Frederick Hilly designed it, and it was named for the Italian Carrara marble used inside. The land had earlier been part of William Charles Wentworth's estate.
The Allen family sells the house to the government and it opens as a women's convalescent home, renamed Strickland House after the NSW Governor, Sir Gerald Strickland.
The convalescent home closes in December, and the estate later passes into public hands as parkland on the Hermitage Foreshore.
Strickland House is listed on the NSW State Heritage Register. It remains a popular film location and public reserve.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Strickland House (originally 'Carrara'), Vaucluse

properties.name: Parramatta Gaol
properties.summary: This grim sandstone gaol stands on O'Connell Street at North Parramatta. The current building was finished in 1842 and held prisoners for close to 170 years, making it one of the longest-serving gaols in Australia by the time it closed in 2011. It was the third gaol built in the town.

timeline.event: Governor Hunter orders Parramatta's first gaol, a timber building near today's Prince Alfred Square. Finished in 1797, it burns down on Christmas Eve that year, killing one prisoner.
Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis draws up a new gaol for Governor Bourke. Work later proceeds to a revised design by the Royal Engineer Captain George Barney, in tooled sandstone.
The sandstone gaol on O'Connell Street, North Parramatta, is completed, and prisoners are moved in during January.
Parramatta Correctional Centre closes on 9 October after close to 170 years. The heritage-listed complex later passes to the Deerubbin Local Aboriginal Land Council.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Parramatta Gaol

properties.name: Old Liverpool Hospital
properties.summary: This is one of the oldest surviving colonial hospital complexes in Australia. The main stone-and-brick building on College Street was begun in 1822 under Governor Macquarie and raised by convict labour, to a design usually attributed to Francis Greenway.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie founds the town of Liverpool. A small brick hospital follows close by, near the Georges River.
Work starts on a much larger stone-and-brick hospital on College Street, built by convict gangs to a design attributed to Francis Greenway.
The main building is finished after about seven years of slow work.
The Benevolent Society takes over the site and opens it as the Liverpool Benevolent Asylum, sheltering aged, infirm and destitute men.
The old building closes as a medical institution. Patients move to the modern Liverpool Hospital nearby, and in 1961 the convict-era building reopens as a technical college, now a TAFE campus.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The old Liverpool Hospital (1829), now a TAFE college

properties.name: Roseville Bridge
properties.summary: This tall concrete bridge carries Warringah Road high over Middle Harbour. It opened in 1966 and replaced a low 1924 crossing that first joined Roseville to the suburbs further north.

timeline.event: The first Roseville Bridge opens on 20 September. Built in reinforced concrete by unemployed returned servicemen, the low two-lane crossing replaced a rowing-boat ferry across Middle Harbour.
Premier Robin Askin opens the present high-level six-lane concrete bridge on 2 April, designed by the Department of Main Roads and built by John Holland.
The old 1924 bridge, by then used only by walkers, is demolished along with the nearby Roseville Baths.

properties.source: Wikipedia; Twentieth Century Heritage Society
properties.image_caption: Roseville Bridge over Middle Harbour

properties.name: Martin Luther Kirche
properties.summary: The Martin Luther Kirche on Goulburn Street was Sydney's first German Lutheran church, consecrated in 1883. More than a century on, it still holds services in German for the city's German community.

timeline.event: A German Evangelical Lutheran congregation forms in Sydney to serve the city's growing German community.
In January the foundation stone is laid on Goulburn Street, to a Victorian Romanesque design by T. Dixon Chater. The wife of the German Consul-General lays the stone.
The church is consecrated on 10 September, the first German Lutheran church built in Sydney, seating around 220 people.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Martin Luther Kirche
properties.image_caption: The Martin Luther Kirche on Goulburn Street

properties.name: Hydraulic Pump Station No. 1
properties.summary: This building was the pumping heart of Sydney's hydraulic power network. It opened in 1891 and used high-pressure water to drive the city's lifts and cranes, years before electric motors took over. At its peak in the 1920s the system was the third largest of its kind in the world, after London and Geneva.

timeline.event: The engineer George Swinburne forms the Sydney and Suburban Hydraulic Power Company.
Pump Station No. 1 opens on Pier Street, pushing high-pressure water through underground mains to work lifts, cranes, wool presses, bank vault doors and theatre machinery across the city.
The network has grown to drive around 720 lifts, having started with fewer than 70 in 1891.
The system winds up after a main near Ultimo is severed during freeway roadworks in 1975. The building survives and later becomes a bar.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The former Hydraulic Pump Station No. 1, Little Pier Street

properties.name: Tusculum
properties.summary: Tusculum is one of John Verge's grand Regency villas on the rise once called Woolloomooloo Hill. It was built in the 1830s under Governor Darling's rules that allowed only fine houses, set in gardens and facing the town.

timeline.event: Governor Darling subdivides Woolloomooloo Hill, now Potts Point, into large blocks under 'villa conditions'. Each grant could carry only one fine house, set in gardens and facing the town.
Tusculum, designed by John Verge for the merchant Alexander Brodie Spark, is finished. Spark lets it to William Grant Broughton, the first Anglican bishop in the colony.

By now decaying and under threat of demolition, Tusculum is taken into government care under the Heritage Act. The Royal Australian Institute of Architects restores it, reopening the villa in 1988 as its NSW home.
The Institute completes a fresh round of repairs, begun in 2011, to deal with stubborn damp in the old walls.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Museums of History NSW
properties.image_caption: Tusculum, John Verge's Regency villa at Potts Point

properties.name: Scots Church
properties.summary: Scots Church was Sydney's first Presbyterian church, founded in the 1820s by the Rev. John Dunmore Lang. Lang was a minister, a tireless champion of Scottish migration, and one of the most combative figures in colonial politics.

timeline.event: The Rev. John Dunmore Lang arrives as the colony's first Presbyterian minister.
The first Scots Church opens on Church Hill, near present-day Wynyard, as Sydney's first Presbyterian church. Its foundation stone had been laid by Governor Brisbane in 1824.
Lang, who had worked hard to bring free Scottish migrants to the colony, is elected to the NSW Legislative Council.
After the original church is resumed and demolished for the Harbour Bridge approaches, the present Gothic Revival building opens on Margaret Street at the corner of York Street.

properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Scots Church, Sydney, c.1931

properties.name: La Perouse Cable Station
properties.summary: This sandstone station on the La Perouse headland was where the undersea telegraph reached Australia, linking the colony to New Zealand and, through it, the rest of the world. For around four decades it kept Sydney connected by cable across Botany Bay's southern shore.

timeline.event: The first submarine telegraph cable between Australia and New Zealand comes ashore at Frenchmans Beach near La Perouse, with the service opening on 21 February.

A permanent stone cable station, designed under Colonial Architect James Barnet, is completed on the headland for the Eastern Extension Telegraph Company, housing both equipment and quarters for the telegraphers.
The company shifts its cable landing to Bondi and shuts the La Perouse station, which later does duty as nurses' quarters and a women's refuge.
The old cable station reopens to the public as the La Perouse Museum.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Randwick City
properties.image_caption: Landing the New Zealand telegraph cable at La Perouse, 1876

properties.name: Manly Art Gallery & Museum
properties.summary: This modest gallery sits on Manly Cove, opened in 1930 inside a converted concert pavilion. It is the oldest purpose-run public art gallery in metropolitan New South Wales.

timeline.event: An open-air concert pavilion, designed by Hedley V. Graham, opens on West Esplanade beside Manly Cove. Sheltered from the wind, it sees little use and is soon dismissed as a white elephant.
The pavilion is enclosed and reopened as the Manly Art Gallery by the Chief Justice, Sir Philip Street, with around 200 works on display, including paintings and historical photographs.

properties.source: Museums & Galleries NSW; Northern Beaches Council
properties.image_caption: The Manly Art Gallery & Museum, West Esplanade

properties.name: Fort Street Model School
properties.summary: This was the colony's first government model school, opened on Observatory Hill in 1849 in a former military hospital. It became a training ground for teachers and the seed of the famous Fort Street selective schools.

timeline.event: The Fort Street Model School opens in the old military hospital on Observatory Hill, on the site of Macquarie's Fort Phillip, as the colony's first government model school for boys and girls.
Under William Wilkins it grows into the colony's school for training teachers and a template for public education across New South Wales.
The school splits into a public primary school and separate boys' and girls' high schools.
Fort Street Boys' High moves to Petersham, short of room on the Hill, and the historic building later passes to the National Trust.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Fort Street school on Observatory Hill

properties.name: Windsor Court House
properties.summary: This Hawkesbury courthouse was designed by Francis Greenway and built by convict labour in 1822 under Governor Macquarie. It is reckoned the oldest courthouse still in use anywhere in Australia, and keeps its original cedar joinery to this day.

timeline.event: Francis Greenway designs a courthouse for Windsor at Macquarie's request, and the building contract goes to William Cox in October for 1800 pounds.
The courthouse is completed by Cox using convict labour, a rare surviving Georgian public building of the early colony.
Colonial Architect James Barnet carries out extensions, part of works he oversaw here through the 1870s and 1880s, while the building stays in use as a courthouse.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Captain Cook Bridge
properties.summary: This concrete bridge of 1965 crosses the Georges River near its mouth, joining Sans Souci to Taren Point. It is the easternmost of the river's crossings, built to retire the old vehicular punt that had ferried cars since 1916.

timeline.event: Construction begins on a high-level bridge across the Georges River close to its mouth, its foundations reaching deep into the sandstone below the water.

The Captain Cook Bridge opens on 29 May, carrying Rocky Point and Taren Point Roads between Sans Souci and Taren Point. It replaces the Taren Point punt, which had moved about 240,000 vehicles in just its final six months.

properties.source: Wikipedia; Transport for NSW
properties.image_caption: The Captain Cook Bridge over the Georges River

properties.name: Woronora Dam
properties.summary: Hidden in the bush south of the Georges River, Woronora Dam was begun in 1927 and finished in 1941. It secured a reliable water supply for the Sutherland Shire and the southern edge of Sydney, and it still serves towns like Helensburgh and Engadine.

timeline.event: Work starts on a curved concrete wall across the Woronora River, designed by chief engineer G. E. Haskins for the Metropolitan Water, Sewerage and Drainage Board, to supply the growing district south of the Georges River.
The Great Depression halts the job, and the workforce scatters to look for other work until unemployment relief money allows building to resume the following year.
Woronora Dam is finished, the last of the dams completed before Warragamba began. To this day it is the only one of Sydney's water supply dams that stands apart from the main interconnected system.

properties.source: WaterNSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Woronora Dam wall

properties.name: People's Palace
properties.summary: The Salvation Army's great temperance hotel stood on Pitt Street, opened in 1899 to give country families and the city poor a cheap, sober bed for the night.

timeline.event: The Salvation Army opens the People's Palace in the old Natatorium building on Pitt Street, a large alcohol-free hotel for country visitors, working people and the poor.
It grows into one of Sydney's biggest hotels, able to put up around 887 guests with a staff of about 80, and becomes the flagship of a chain of Salvation Army People's Palaces.
The building is demolished, though its facade is kept and worked into the later development on the site.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; The Salvation Army

properties.name: Liverpool Weir
properties.summary: Convict gangs raised this stone weir across the Georges River at Liverpool in 1836, working to a design by David Lennox, the colony's bridge engineer. It held back the salt tide and gave the town its first reliable fresh water, a job it did until about 1880.

timeline.event: Over a few months in 1836, ironed convict gangs supervised by Captain William Christie quarry and cut the stone and build the weir to David Lennox's design, keeping the tide out and supplying Liverpool with fresh water.
A fishway is added so that native fish can once again move past the old weir.

Long recognised as one of the earliest surviving colonial engineered weirs in the country, the weir remains a landmark on the Georges River. (Its State Heritage Register listing was actually gazetted back in 1999.)

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Liverpool Weir on the Georges River

properties.name: Caerleon
properties.summary: Caerleon is the Bellevue Hill mansion that introduced the Queen Anne style to Sydney. Built from 1885 for the Sydney Morning Herald proprietor Charles Burton Fairfax, its red brick and busy gables set the pattern for the Federation houses that later filled the suburbs.

timeline.event: Work starts on Caerleon for Charles Burton Fairfax, a grandson of the Herald's founder. The Sydney architect Harry Kent drew the first plans, only to see them reworked in London by Maurice Adams and his own name dropped when the drawings were exhibited there.
The house is finished in red brick with stone dressings, tall chimneys and terracotta shingles. As the earliest Queen Anne Revival house in the city, it shapes a generation of domestic architecture.

properties.source: NSW heritage; National Trust
properties.image_caption: Caerleon, Bellevue Hill

properties.name: The Mariners' Church
properties.summary: This sandstone church on Lower George Street at the Rocks was built in the late 1850s by the Sydney Bethel Union to care for the sailors who crowded the port. It stood at the heart of the city's maritime quarter, a quiet refuge for seafarers far from home.

timeline.event: The Sydney Bethel Union forms to minister to the sailors visiting the harbour, part of a wider evangelical movement reaching out to seamen.
The Union is granted a site near Circular Quay and work begins on its new church, designed by the architect John Bibb in a restrained classical style.
The church opens in February, serving the seafarers of the last great age of sail.
The building is altered and soon extended for the Rawson Institute for Seamen. It still stands today in commercial use.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The former Mariners' Church (Rawson Institute), the Rocks

properties.name: St Philip's Church Hill
properties.summary: St Philip's is the oldest Anglican parish in Australia, begun in 1793 by the colony's first chaplain. Its present sandstone Gothic church, designed by Edmund Blacket, has crowned Church Hill above York Street since 1856.

timeline.event: The Rev. Richard Johnson, the colony's first chaplain, has a simple church built at the corner of Bligh and Hunter Streets. It is the first church in Australia and the start of its oldest parish.
Convicts burn that first church to the ground.
A second church, this time in stone with a tall round tower, is built on Church Hill under Governor Macquarie. It was later ridiculed as one of the ugliest churches in Christendom.
The present church opens, a sandstone Gothic Revival design by Edmund Blacket whose tower echoes Magdalen College, Oxford.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: St Philip's Church, Church Hill

properties.name: Bushells Building
properties.summary: This seven-storey tea works on Harrington Street at the Rocks is where Bushells blended, packed and sent out the tea that became a fixture of the Australian kitchen. Built in the 1920s, it kept its tea-tasting room and a tangle of chutes and lifts that moved the leaf from floor to floor.

timeline.event: Alfred Bushell opens a tea shop in Brisbane. His sons Walter and Philip later carry the business into Sydney, trading as Bushells from the turn of the century.
The grand seven-storey factory is completed at 121-127 Harrington Street, blending and packing tea for the whole country.
Long after Bushells moved its plant out to Concord, the building is added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 10 May.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Bushells Building, Harrington Street, the Rocks

properties.name: The Witches' Houses
properties.summary: Along the Johnston Street ridge at Annandale stands a row of turreted Gothic mansions built in the 1880s by John Young, the master builder behind St Mary's Cathedral. The houses with spired roofs picked up the nickname the Witches' Houses, after the witches' hats their towers seemed to wear.

timeline.event: John Young buys land near Rozelle Bay, dreaming of a grand suburb to rival Sydney's most exclusive addresses, and begins his row of idiosyncratic houses along the ridge.

The Abbey, the most elaborate of them, is finished in sandstone with a copper-clad tower, a cloister and carved gargoyles. Nearby Kenilworth, where Sir Henry Parkes spent his last years and died in 1896, joins the famous spired group with Highroyd and Hockindon.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Abbey, one of the Witches' Houses of Annandale

properties.name: Swifts
properties.summary: Swifts is a castellated Gothic Revival mansion at Darling Point, raised by the brewing money of Robert Lucas-Tooth of Tooth and Co. The Australian Heritage Council once called it perhaps the grandest house left in Sydney.

timeline.event: Robert Lucas-Tooth, of the Tooth and Co brewing family, builds his first house here after buying the Darling Point land in the early 1870s.

He has the house remade as a sandstone Gothic pile, with battlements, a tower and an arcade, to designs by the architect Gustavus Alphonse Morrell. Morrell borrowed touches from Sydney's Government House, and the result echoed the family seat, Great Swifts in Cranbrook, Kent.
The brewer Edmund Resch junior dies and leaves Swifts to the Sisters of Charity and the Catholic Church. From 1964 it became the home of Sydney's Catholic archbishops, among them Cardinal Gilroy.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Swifts, Darling Point

properties.name: Fernhill
properties.summary: Fernhill is a grand Greek Revival mansion in the Mulgoa Valley, built in local sandstone for the Cox family in the early 1840s. Twenty Irish stonemasons were shipped out under the bounty scheme to raise it, and the estate later became famous as a nursery of Australian racehorses.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie confirms a Mulgoa Valley grant held in the name of William Cox's young son Edward, anchoring the family in the valley.
Fernhill, a great Greek Revival sandstone house begun the year before for Edward Cox, is completed using stone quarried on the estate and worked by Irish masons brought out for the job.
Edward King Cox, who inherited in 1863, has by now made the family's racing stud one of the colony's finest, breeding Melbourne Cup winners and the first Sydney Cup winner.
After generations in private hands, the heritage estate is bought by the New South Wales Government.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: All Saints' Church, Hunters Hill
properties.summary: John Horbury Hunt, a master of rugged Gothic in brick and stone, designed this quarry-faced sandstone church at Hunters Hill, said to be his only church in Sydney. Its prize is a set of memorial windows drawn by Edward Burne-Jones and made by Morris and Co., the only glass of its kind in the city.

timeline.event: The Bishop of Sydney lays the foundation stone on 30 May. The architect is John Horbury Hunt, who would later be dismissed after insisting on tile rather than lead capping for the roof.
The quarry-faced sandstone church opens on 22 April, reputedly Horbury Hunt's only church in Sydney itself.

A tower, an extra bay to the nave and a narthex are added. By this time the church already holds its Burne-Jones and Morris and Co. memorial windows, installed as tributes to local men, including the Simpson window dedicated in 1926.

properties.source: NSW heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: All Saints' Church, Hunters Hill

properties.name: Cleveland House
properties.summary: This two-storey stuccoed Georgian mansion on Chalmers Street is reckoned the oldest surviving residence in Sydney. It was built for Daniel Cooper, an emancipist who rose to become one of the colony's wealthiest merchants, on his Cleveland Paddocks estate.

timeline.event: Daniel Cooper, an emancipist turned wealthy merchant, takes up the Cleveland Paddocks on the southern edge of the town.

Cleveland House is built as Cooper's residence, a broad Colonial Georgian villa. The design is traditionally attributed to convict architect Francis Greenway, though no document proves it, and Cooper is said to have spent four thousand pounds on the work.
Cooper dies, and within two years the surrounding paddocks are carved up into the dense terraces of Surry Hills, leaving the house marooned among them.
Cleveland House is added to the NSW State Heritage Register as a rare survivor of Sydney's earliest domestic architecture.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Cleveland House, Surry Hills

properties.name: Hughenden
properties.summary: Hughenden is a grand Victorian Italianate mansion on Queen Street, Woollahra, raised about 1876 for Dr Frederick Harrison Quaife, a leading colonial physician who is credited with bringing the first X-ray to the colony. After the Quaifes it drifted through a string of lives before the Gervay family rescued it as a boutique hotel.

timeline.event: Hughenden is built for Dr Frederick Harrison Quaife, a founder of the British Medical Association in the colony and son of Barzillai Quaife, often called Australia's first philosopher. The doctor's initials are still etched in Lombardic script in the glass above the front door.
With the Quaife years over, the house turns its hand to almost anything. It serves in turn as a Masonic hall, a guest house, a nurses' home, a dance hall and the Riviere ladies' college.
By now run down, the mansion is bought and carefully restored by the Gervay family, who reopen it as the Hughenden boutique hotel.

properties.source: Woollahra heritage; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Hordern Pavilion
properties.summary: The Hordern Pavilion is a big brick exhibition hall at Moore Park, built for the Royal Agricultural Society to give the Easter Show more room indoors. It opened in 1924 and later became one of Sydney's best-loved venues for live music and dance parties.

timeline.event: Work begins on a grand new pavilion on the Sydney Showground at Moore Park, designed by the firm Trenchard Smith and Maisey in a restrained classical style.

Premier Sir George Fuller opens the pavilion on 2 April, at a cost of around forty-five thousand pounds. It is named for the Hordern retail family, and for Samuel Hordern, the Royal Agricultural Society's president of long standing.
With its showground days fading, the pavilion reinvents itself as a rock and dance-party hall, going on to host generations of touring acts.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Hordern Pavilion, Moore Park

properties.name: Lyndhurst
properties.summary: Lyndhurst is a refined Colonial Regency villa in Glebe, designed by the architect John Verge for the colony's principal surgeon, Dr James Bowman. A builders' green ban saved it from a 1970s freeway, and a careful restoration later turned it into a heritage headquarters.

timeline.event: Dr James Bowman, the principal colonial surgeon and a son-in-law of John Macarthur, buys the land and commissions a villa from the fashionable architect John Verge.
Lyndhurst is finished, a polished two-storey Regency villa on 36 acres. In later decades it is put to many uses, serving as a college, a hospital and even a factory.
With the house marked for demolition to clear a path for the proposed North-Western expressway, the Save Lyndhurst Committee wins a Builders Labourers Federation green ban that stops the bulldozers.
After a careful restoration led by Clive Lucas, Lyndhurst reopens as a heritage headquarters and home of the Historic Houses Trust. It joins the State Heritage Register in 1999.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Lyndhurst, Glebe (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0)

properties.name: Goldsbrough Mort Wool Store
properties.summary: This huge brick wool warehouse on the Pyrmont peninsula was built in 1883 for Goldsbrough Mort and Co, at the height of the wool trade that ran on Sydney's wealth. It was the first of around twenty wool stores to crowd the peninsula, and it was later turned into apartments.

timeline.event: Goldsbrough Mort and Co open their No. 1 Wool Store, designed by William Pritchard and built by the Stuart brothers. It could hold some 50,000 bales and was the first of around twenty wool stores that came to line the Pyrmont peninsula.
A fire takes hold and burns through the store for almost two weeks. The building is rebuilt behind its original facade.
The redundant store is converted into apartments, keeping its solid Victorian exterior as Pyrmont turns residential.

properties.source: City of Sydney heritage; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Goldsbrough Mort wool store, Pyrmont

properties.name: Lindesay
properties.summary: Lindesay is an early Gothic Revival villa at Darling Point. It was the first house built on the point and the earliest picturesque Gothic villa in Australia, and the National Trust now keeps it as a house museum.

timeline.event: Building begins for Campbell Drummond Riddell, the newly arrived Colonial Treasurer. Lindesay is the first house to rise on Darling Point after the point is cut up into suburban allotments.
The house is finished in an early Gothic Revival style, with battlemented parapets that mark a romantic break from the plain Georgian taste of the day.
The last private owner, Walter Pye, gives Lindesay to the National Trust of Australia (NSW), which runs it as a house museum and functions venue. It is added to the State Heritage Register in 1999.

properties.source: National Trust (NSW); NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Lindesay, Darling Point

properties.name: Henry Head Battery
properties.summary: Henry Head Battery is a coastal artillery battery on the headland at La Perouse, built in the 1890s with disappearing guns to guard the entrance to Botany Bay. Its emplacements now survive as ruins within Kamay Botany Bay National Park.

timeline.event: Work begins on a battery at Henry Head, near Cape Banks, to defend the northern shore of the Botany Bay entrance.

The battery is finished with two BL 6-inch disappearing guns that dropped out of sight to reload. It formed part of the ring of forts defending Sydney's southern approaches, separate from the island fort at Bare Island just along the shore.
The old battery, redundant since about 1910, is brought back into use with field guns during the Second World War. Today its magazines and gun pits remain as ruins in the national park.

properties.source: NPWS; NSW heritage
properties.image_caption: Henry Head Battery, La Perouse (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

properties.name: Glenalvon
properties.summary: Glenalvon is a handsome Colonial Georgian sandstone house in Campbelltown, built around 1841 by the local publican Michael Byrne. It is one of the finest early houses in Sydney's south-west and now serves as a house museum.

timeline.event: Michael Byrne, a Campbelltown publican and son of an Irish rebel transported after the 1798 rising, builds the two-storey sandstone house with its stone Doric verandah columns. An earlier servants' wing survives behind it.
A Victorian coach house and stables are added to the grounds as the property fills out.
Owned by Campbelltown City Council and run as a museum by the Campbelltown and Airds Historical Society, Glenalvon is added to the NSW State Heritage Register.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Campbelltown & Airds Historical Society
properties.image_caption: Glenalvon House, Campbelltown

properties.name: St John's Cathedral, Parramatta
properties.summary: This twin-towered Anglican cathedral stands on the oldest continuously used church site in Australia. Its tall western spires have marked the heart of Parramatta since the Macquarie years.

timeline.event: The foundation stone of the colony's first brick church was laid at Parramatta in 1797. The parish church of St John's was named for Governor Hunter, and the first service followed in April 1803.

Governor Macquarie ordered the building of the twin western towers, and his officer Lieutenant John Watts drew up the design. One folktale holds that Elizabeth Macquarie modelled them on the church at Reculver in Kent, the last she saw as she left England. The towers were finished in 1819.
The old church was pulled down and a new sandstone nave built by the Parramatta architect James Houison, keeping the original towers. The firm of E. T. Blacket and Son added the transepts from 1883.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: St John's Cathedral, Parramatta

properties.name: Arms of Australia Inn
properties.summary: This colonial wayside inn at Emu Plains is among the oldest buildings in the Penrith district. It gave travellers a last rest before the hard climb west over the Blue Mountains, and it now serves as a local history museum.

timeline.event: The earliest part of the building went up at Emu Plains around 1826, on the western road at the foot of the Blue Mountains. Heritage records date the fabric across a range to about 1840.

The land was split in two in 1840, and John Mortimer took half. He became the inn's first licensee the following year, running one of about two dozen roadside inns of the Nepean that watered travellers and teamsters bound for Bathurst and, later, the goldfields.
The Nepean District Historical Society saved the inn from demolition in 1976 and opened it as a local history museum, which it remains.

properties.source: NSW heritage; Nepean District Historical Society
properties.image_caption: Arms of Australia Inn, Emu Plains

properties.name: St Luke's Church, Liverpool
properties.summary: This is Francis Greenway's church at Liverpool, with its foundation stone laid by Governor Macquarie in 1818. It is often called the oldest surviving Anglican church in Australia.

timeline.event: Governor Macquarie laid the foundation stone of St Luke's on 7 April 1818. The convict architect Francis Greenway drew the design, and Nathaniel Lucas built it.
The first service was held on St Luke's Day, 18 October 1819. The church was substantially complete by then, with fitting-out carrying on to about 1820.

During Moore College's centenary in 1956, someone realised the church had never actually been consecrated. The oversight was put right with a consecration on 10 March, 137 years after the first service.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: St Luke's Church, Liverpool

properties.name: Old Liverpool Courthouse
properties.summary: This single-storey Colonial Georgian building in Bigge Street, Liverpool, began around 1820 as the town's convict gaol house. It later served as a courthouse for well over a century, and is thought to be one of the oldest surviving convict barracks on the mainland.

timeline.event: Built under Governor Macquarie about 1820 as Liverpool's gaol house, in sandstock brick on a sandstone base. It is said to be the only courthouse in New South Wales never designed by the Government Architect.

The building was adapted as a courthouse, a role it filled for more than 120 years. A police station was later added at the rear, and over the years it also served as a schoolhouse, a place of worship and a town hall.
After long service in colonial and later justice, the old courthouse was added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 3 November 2017.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The old Liverpool Courthouse (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0)

properties.name: Studley Park House
properties.summary: This grand Victorian Italianate mansion above Narellan went up in 1889 and cost so much that it ruined its builder, earning the nickname Payne's Folly. Its later years are shadowed by the drowning of a schoolboy whose ghost story still clings to the place.

timeline.event: The businessman William Charles Payne built Studley Park in 1889, a richly detailed Italianate mansion designed by the Sydney architects A. L. and G. McCredie. The expense helped to ruin him, and he sold up in 1902.
The house became Camden Grammar School, a boys' school that ran here until 1933.

On 15 October 1909 a fourteen-year-old pupil, Ray Blackstone, drowned in the estate dam. That tragedy, along with the house's lonely setting, gave rise to lasting ghost stories. The mansion later served as the army's Eastern Command Training School during the Second World War, and it is today the Camden Golf Club clubhouse.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Camden History Notes
properties.image_caption: Studley Park House, c.1900

properties.name: Steele Point Battery
properties.summary: Steele Point Battery is a colonial gun battery cut into the sandstone point at Nielsen Park, Vaucluse. It is one of the most intact survivors of the 1870s works that ringed Sydney Harbour with guns after Britain pulled its troops out of the colony.

timeline.event: With the imperial garrison withdrawn in 1870, the colony starts building a ring of inner-harbour batteries. Work begins at Steel Point under Colonial Architect James Barnet, alongside Middle Head, Georges Head and Bradleys Head.
Three 80-pounder rifled muzzle-loading guns are installed in sandstone emplacements, linked by passages to an underground powder magazine.
The battery is finished, with barracks added in 1880. It was later rearmed with 5-inch breech-loaders, and its emplacements and tunnels survive today within Sydney Harbour National Park.

properties.source: NSW NPWS; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Steele Point, Nielsen Park (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

properties.name: Hawkesbury River Railway Bridge
properties.summary: This 1889 rail bridge near Brooklyn was the last link that joined Sydney's railways to the rest of eastern Australia. It was a celebrated piece of engineering, but a cracked pier eventually forced a replacement bridge that opened in 1946.

timeline.event: The Union Bridge Company of New York wins the contract in January to span the broad, deep Hawkesbury. Its caisson foundations are sunk to record depths of around 46 to 49 metres below high water.

The bridge opens on 1 May, completing the rail line between Sydney and Newcastle. For the first time the railways of New South Wales, Queensland, Victoria and South Australia are joined by continuous track.
A severe crack found in a pier in 1938 had made replacement urgent. A new bridge, set about 60 metres upstream, opens on 1 July, and the old sandstone-capped piers are left standing in the river.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; engineering histories
properties.image_caption: The original Hawkesbury River Railway Bridge, 1888

properties.name: Gledswood Homestead
properties.summary: Gledswood is a colonial homestead and early vineyard in Sydney's south-west, near Catherine Field. Its earliest structures were convict-built, and the Chisholm family held it for some ninety years. New suburbs have since grown up around it, and today it works as a wedding and function venue.

timeline.event: A 400-acre grant is made to Gabriel Louis Marie Huon de Kerilleau, a French emigre who had fled the Revolution and joined the New South Wales Corps. The earliest stone buildings on the estate, then called Buckingham, are raised with convict labour.

The merchant James Chisholm, who had bought the estate in 1816 for 250 pounds and renamed it Gledswood, completes the stuccoed-stone homestead in a low Indian-bungalow style. His son plants a vineyard about this time, and German vinedressers Frederick and Anna Worner are brought out in 1847 to work it. A convict-built cellar beneath the house could hold some twenty thousand bottles.
After about ninety years in the Chisholm family and a later revival of its winery, Gledswood is added to the NSW State Heritage Register. It now operates as a wedding and function venue.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Camden History Notes
properties.image_caption: Gledswood Homestead, near Camden (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 4.0)

properties.name: HMAS Watson
properties.summary: HMAS Watson sits on the tip of South Head, where the navy ran a wartime radar school under the cover name HMAS Radar. It is now the navy's Maritime Warfare School, training crews to read and fight the sea around them.

timeline.event: The navy shifts its radar school from HMAS Rushcutter out to South Head, where it works quietly under the cover name HMAS Radar.
The base is formally commissioned as HMAS Watson on 14 March. The name nods to Watsons Bay below, but also honours Sir Robert Watson-Watt, one of the pioneers of radar.
Torpedo and anti-submarine warfare training move to Watson, and it grows into the navy's main maritime-warfare school, set among the old harbour-defence works on the headland.

properties.source: RAN Sea Power Centre; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: HMAS Watson, South Head

properties.name: Currawong
properties.summary: On the wooded western shore of Pittwater sits Currawong, a union-built holiday camp that has survived where almost every other workers' resort vanished. It carries the mid-century idea that ordinary families deserved an affordable holiday by the water, and a long community campaign saved it from the developers.

timeline.event: The homestead called Midholme is built at Little Mackerel Beach, on a stretch of shore reachable only by water. Its part-fibro walls were among the earliest uses of the material in the district.
The Labor Council of NSW buys the property for ten thousand pounds as a holiday camp for union members, a dividend of post-war wins like paid annual leave and the forty-hour week.
Simple prefabricated cabins go up, with more following in 1953, each named after a native bird or animal. For decades working families holiday here, arriving by ferry from Palm Beach.
After the unions sold the site to a developer in 2007, a determined community campaign persuades the NSW Government to buy it back. It is heritage-listed, and in 2015 it is declared a state park.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Pittwater Online News
properties.image_caption: Currawong cottages, Pittwater (Wikimedia Commons, CC0)

properties.name: Yiu Ming Temple
properties.summary: A traditional Chinese village temple sits quietly among the warehouses of Alexandria, built around 1909 by migrants from the Gaoyao and Gaoming districts of Guangdong. It is one of the oldest surviving Chinese temples in Australia, and a rare intact example of its kind anywhere.

timeline.event: The Yiu Ming Society, whose name joins the second syllables of Gaoyao and Gaoming, finishes its temple in Retreat Street. The figurative ceramic roof tiles are shipped out from the Foshan kilns in Guangdong.
Further work completes the rich interior. The temple honours Hung Shing, a god of the southern seas, and remains the only temple in Australia dedicated to him.
As a rare window into the life of Sydney's early Chinese community, the temple is added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 24 September.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Yiu Ming Temple, Alexandria

properties.name: HMAS Penguin
properties.summary: HMAS Penguin is the Royal Australian Navy's base at Balmoral, tucked into the Middle Head side of the harbour. It began as a wartime depot, spent two decades hosting visiting submarines, and is now the navy's school for divers, hydrographers and medics.

timeline.event: The Balmoral Naval Depot is commissioned in July as Penguin II, working as an annexe to the main base at Garden Island. It is renamed HMAS Penguin in January 1943.
The Royal Navy's Fourth Submarine Squadron, based at Penguin from 1949, makes Balmoral the home of submarines in Sydney. The boats stay until the squadron disbands in January 1969.
Penguin settles into its modern shape as home of the ADF Diving School, the navy's Hydrographic and Medical schools, and the Balmoral Naval Hospital, which served until the mid-2000s.

properties.source: RAN Sea Power Centre; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: HMAS Penguin, from Balmoral Beach

properties.name: Tresillian, Petersham
properties.summary: This Petersham house gave its name to Tresillian, the mothercraft service born out of the Royal Society for the Welfare of Mothers and Babies. The Society set out to bring down a heartbreaking infant death rate, and the work it began here still carries on across New South Wales.

timeline.event: With infant deaths climbing through the war years and the looming influenza pandemic, the Royal Society for the Welfare of Mothers and Babies is founded at a meeting in Sydney Town Hall.

The Society opens its first infant welfare training home in a Shaw Street house already called Tresillian, named by earlier owners after a Cornish village. Dr Margaret Harper, the first Medical Director, runs it, and the name soon stands for the whole movement, with Queen Mary as patron.
By now the nurses, rest homes and pure milk campaigns have reshaped infant health right across the state, and the service keeps going today.

properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Garvan Institute of Medical Research
properties.summary: The Garvan Institute is one of Australia's foremost medical research bodies, founded in 1963 beside St Vincent's Hospital in Darlinghurst. It has grown into a powerhouse for work in genomics, cancer and immunology.

timeline.event: The Sisters of Charity open the Garvan Institute as a research arm of St Vincent's Hospital, funded by a hospital centenary appeal. At the request of its largest donor, Helen Mills, it is named for her father, the parliamentarian James Patrick Garvan.
The Kinghorn Cancer Centre, a 100 million dollar joint venture with St Vincent's, is opened in the Darlinghurst health precinct by Prime Minister Julia Gillard.
Garvan becomes one of the first places in the world able to sequence a human genome for under 1,000 US dollars, putting it at the leading edge of medical genomics.

properties.source: Garvan Institute; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Garvan Institute, Darlinghurst

properties.name: Chief Secretary's Building
properties.summary: This richly carved sandstone palace on the corner of Macquarie and Bridge Streets was the seat of colonial administration in New South Wales. From here the Colonial Secretary's department, later the Chief Secretary's, ran much of the business of nineteenth-century government.

timeline.event: Colonial Architect James Barnet starts work on the Colonial Secretary's Building, a grand sandstone home for the department in the Victorian Free Classical manner.
Barnet's first four storeys are finished, fronting Macquarie and Bridge Streets. Allegorical figures of Mercy, Justice and Wisdom look down from the facade.

Walter Liberty Vernon completes a second stage begun in 1894, adding a fifth-floor mansard and a corner dome. The dome was clad in aluminium, one of the earliest uses of the metal anywhere in the world, and government has occupied the building ever since.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Chief Secretary's Building, Sydney

properties.name: The Argyle Stores
properties.summary: The Argyle Stores are a cluster of sturdy sandstone bond stores in The Rocks, begun in 1826 and counted among Sydney's oldest commercial buildings. Behind their thick walls, imported spirits, tea and tobacco sat locked away under customs bond until the duty was paid.

timeline.event: Captain John Piper begins a building on Argyle Street, the future east wing. He sells it before it is finished, and by 1828 it has passed to Mary Reibey and then to Frederick Unwin.

The complex grows with new wings over the following years. Having served from 1830 as one of Sydney's earliest customs houses, it works as a bonded store holding brandy, wine, tea, tobacco and sugar awaiting duty.
As one of the oldest commercial complexes in Sydney, the Argyle Stores are added to the NSW State Heritage Register. The buildings now hold offices, bars and a restaurant.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Argyle Stores, The Rocks

properties.name: Admiralty House
properties.summary: John Gibbes, the colony's Collector of Customs, built this single-storey harbourside house on Kirribilli Point and named it Wotonga. After the Royal Navy made it the home of its Australian commander it was renamed Admiralty House, and today it is the Governor-General's official Sydney residence.

timeline.event: John Gibbes, Collector of Customs, finishes a graceful single-storey house with wide verandahs on Kirribilli Point, looking back across the harbour to the town, and names it Wotonga.

The colonial government buys Wotonga as the residence of the Royal Navy's Commander-in-Chief of the Australia Station. A second storey and a colonnaded verandah are added, and the house is renamed Admiralty House.

The last British admiral departs as naval defence passes to the Royal Australian Navy, and the house goes to the Commonwealth as the Governor-General's residence. It has been the Governor-General's Sydney home since 1935, next door to Kirribilli House.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Government House / Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Admiralty House, Kirribilli

properties.name: Hotel Australia
properties.summary: For eighty years the Hotel Australia was the place to stay and be seen in Sydney, an address grand enough that the actress Sarah Bernhardt opened it and signed first in the register. It fell in 1971 to clear the block for Harry Seidler's MLC Centre.

timeline.event: The Hotel Australia opens on Castlereagh Street and is at once the most fashionable hotel in the city, calling itself 'the Hotel of the Commonwealth'. Sarah Bernhardt performs the opening and her name heads the register.
An Art Deco extension by the architect Emil Sodersten reaches north to Martin Place, all black marble and silver, with a wing also opening onto the little laneway of Rowe Street.
After eighty years at the heart of Sydney society the hotel closes on 30 June and comes down within months, clearing the site for Harry Seidler's MLC Centre.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: The Hotel Australia, Castlereagh Street, c.1892

properties.name: Sydney Football Stadium
properties.summary: This arena beside the Sydney Cricket Ground sits on the old Sydney Sports Ground at Moore Park. Philip Cox's sweeping 1988 stadium won wide praise, yet it was knocked down and rebuilt barely thirty years later.

timeline.event: The Sydney Sports Ground opens at Moore Park, declared open by Governor Sir Harry Rawson on 3 October. For decades it hosts athletics, cycling and rugby league as the quieter neighbour of the great cricket ground next door.

The Sydney Football Stadium opens in January, a cantilevered design by Philip Cox. It becomes home to the Sydney Roosters, the NSW Waratahs and, later, Sydney FC. Allianz took the naming rights in 2012.
The state government's plan to knock down and rebuild a stadium barely thirty years old turns into a political flashpoint over public spending. The ground closes that October.
Demolition runs through the year and is finished by December.
A new Sydney Football Stadium, again carrying the Allianz name, opens on the same site in August.

stories: For most of the twentieth century this was the Sydney Sports Ground, sharing the great cricket ground's crowds and gates with the foot-runners, cyclists and footballers who filled it.
properties.image_caption: The original Sydney Football Stadium (1988), Philip Cox's design, beside the SCG (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0)
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Trove; Wikipedia

properties.name: The Sydney Showground
properties.summary: From 1882 the Royal Agricultural Society held its show on this ground at Moore Park, beside the cricket ground, and the Royal Easter Show grew into the city's great yearly meeting of country and town. From 1926 the same arena roared at night as the Sydney Showground Speedway. The last Show was held here in 1997, and the site became Fox Studios and the Entertainment Quarter.

timeline.event: The Royal Agricultural Society moves its show to a new ground at Moore Park, next to the cricket ground, and the Royal Easter Show takes root as Sydney's biggest annual gathering.
The Royal Hall of Industries opens to display the colony's goods and produce. The Hordern Pavilion joins it in April 1924, and both halls still stand.
Motorcycle racing begins on the main arena as the Sydney Showground Speedway, soon known as the Speedway Royale. It packs in crowds on summer nights and runs for seventy years.
The last Royal Easter Show is held at Moore Park before the event moves to a new home at Homebush the following year.
The site is redeveloped as Fox Studios Australia, with the public Entertainment Quarter alongside, keeping the heritage Hall of Industries and Hordern Pavilion.

stories: For generations the Show was the one week when the bush came to the city, prize bulls, woodchopping, sideshow alley and the dagwood dog, all crammed onto the ground next door to the cricket.
On speedway nights the arena that hosted the Show by day roared with motorcycles and midget cars, one of Sydney's great Saturday-night institutions.
properties.image_caption: The Sydney Showground beside the cricket ground, 1936 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Trove; Royal Agricultural Society

properties.summary: This little Gothic crypt holds William Charles Wentworth, the explorer, newspaper founder and campaigner often called the father of self-government in New South Wales. It sits on a sandstone rise above Vaucluse House, on the estate he made the grandest private domain on the harbour.

timeline.event: Wentworth begins building his estate at Vaucluse, turning the harbourside land into the most imposing private property in the colony.
Wentworth dies in England. His dying wish is to be buried on his own land at Vaucluse, so his body is shipped home to the colony he had done so much to shape.

On 6 May the colony gives Wentworth its first state funeral. A procession more than a mile long follows his coffin out to Vaucluse, where he is laid to rest on his estate while his sandstone tomb is still being finished by the architects Mansfield Brothers.
The mausoleum and its private right of way, Chapel Road, survive as a monument to the man who pushed the colony towards governing itself.

stories: Wentworth's was the first state funeral held in Australia: on 6 May 1873 shops closed, flags flew at half-mast, and a procession over a mile long followed his coffin from the city to Vaucluse, where he was laid in the mausoleum on his own estate.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register
properties.name: Wentworth Mausoleum
properties.image_caption: The Gothic Wentworth Mausoleum at Vaucluse

properties.name: Anzac Bridge
properties.architect: Ray Wedgwood (Chief Bridge Engineer, Roads and Traffic Authority)
properties.original_use: Road bridge carrying the Western Distributor over Johnstons Bay
properties.summary: The Anzac Bridge is a cable-stayed road bridge over Johnstons Bay between Pyrmont and Rozelle, opened in 1995 with a 345 metre main span that remains the longest of its kind in Australia. It was renamed in 1998 to honour the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps.

timeline.event: The electrically operated Glebe Island swing bridge opens across Johnstons Bay, carrying road traffic on the route the new bridge would later replace.
The cable-stayed bridge opens on 3 December, built by Baulderstone at a cost of about 170 million dollars, carrying the Western Distributor on a 345 metre main span hung from two 120 metre towers.
Premier Bob Carr renames the bridge the Anzac Bridge on Remembrance Day, 11 November, in memory of the Australian and New Zealand soldiers of the First World War.
A four metre bronze statue of an Australian digger by sculptor Alan Somerville is unveiled at the western end on Anzac Day.
A matching statue of a New Zealand soldier, also by Alan Somerville, is unveiled on 27 April by New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark.

properties.source: Wikipedia, NSW War Memorials Register, Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The two great bridges from Glebe Point

properties.name: Bare Island Fort
properties.architect: Peter Scratchley and Gustave Morell, under Colonial Architect James Barnet
properties.original_use: Coastal defence fort guarding Botany Bay
properties.current_use: Historic site within Kamay Botany Bay National Park, open for guided tours
properties.summary: Bare Island Fort is a low concrete fortification built on a rocky islet at La Perouse to guard the southern approach to Sydney through Botany Bay. It was raised in the 1880s against a feared Russian attack that never came, and its guns were never fired in anger.

timeline.event: Work begins on the fort in April, with John McLeod contracted to build a design shaped by Peter Scratchley and Gustave Morell.
The main fortification is completed at a cost of about 34,000 pounds, though no Russian fleet ever appears in Botany Bay.
A Board of Inquiry finds the concrete work faulty and blames Colonial Architect James Barnet, hastening his retirement from public life.
The fort opens as a home for war veterans, the first of its kind in Australia, with the first seven residents moving in.
The veterans are moved out and the island returns to military use during the Second World War.
The veterans home closes after the residents are relocated.
Bare Island is gazetted as a historic site and passes to the National Parks and Wildlife Service.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney and Wikipedia (Bare Island, NSW), cross-checked with NPWS and La Perouse Museum.
properties.image_caption: Bare Island fort, La Perouse

properties.name: Cadmans Cottage
properties.original_use: Barracks for the government coxswains and their boat crews
properties.current_use: Heritage site and visitor information centre
properties.summary: Cadmans Cottage is the second oldest surviving residential building in Sydney, raised on the edge of Sydney Cove in 1816 for the government boat crews. It takes its name from John Cadman, an ex-convict who became Superintendent of Boats and lived here from the late 1820s.

timeline.event: The cottage is completed on the shore of Sydney Cove as barracks for the government coxswains and their crews.
John Cadman, a pardoned convict serving as Superintendent of Boats, takes up residence with his family.
The Sydney Water Police occupy the building and use it as their headquarters.
The building is handed over to the Sydney Sailors Home for the welfare of seamen.
Reclamation of the foreshore for Circular Quay fills the land in front of the cottage, leaving it stranded back from the water.
The cottage is proclaimed a historic site and later opens to the public as a heritage centre.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney, NSW State Heritage Register, NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service.
properties.image_caption: Cadman's Cottage today, the harbour once lapped metres from its door

properties.name: Cockatoo Island (Wareamah)
properties.current_use: Heritage and cultural site with tours, art events and camping
properties.summary: Cockatoo Island, known to Aboriginal people as Wareamah, became a convict penal establishment in 1839 where prisoners cut grain silos and the Fitzroy Dock into the sandstone. It grew into Australia's largest shipyard, building and repairing ships through both world wars before closing in 1991.

timeline.event: Governor George Gipps establishes a convict penal settlement on the island, where prisoners begin cutting grain silos into the rock.
The Fitzroy Dock, designed by civil engineer Gother Kerr Mann and built with convict labour, is completed after about ten years of work.
Convict use ends and the prison closes as the men are moved off the island.
The larger Sutherland Dock, engineered by Louis Samuel, opens to handle the biggest ships of the day.
The island is transferred to the Commonwealth and becomes the Royal Australian Navy's main dockyard.
The dockyard closes after more than 130 years of shipbuilding and repair.
The convict site is inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as part of the Australian Convict Sites.

properties.source: Cockatoo Island (Harbour Trust), Wikipedia, Australian Heritage Database
properties.image_caption: Ferries in the convict-built Fitzroy Dock, Cockatoo Island, 1926

properties.name: Dawes Point Battery
properties.architect: Francis Greenway (1819 rebuild), George Barney (later works)
properties.current_use: Archaeological site and public parkland beneath the Harbour Bridge
properties.summary: Dawes Point Battery was Sydney's first permanent fortification, built in 1791 on the headland the Gadigal knew as Tar-ra. Most of it was demolished in 1925 for the Sydney Harbour Bridge, and its excavated remains now sit in parkland under the southern pylon.

timeline.event: Lieutenant William Dawes built a small observatory and weather station on the point, the first in the colony.
While living on the point, Dawes recorded the Sydney language in his notebooks with a young Gadigal woman, Patyegarang.
A gun battery was built on the site amid fears of a Spanish attack, making it Sydney's first permanent fortification.
Francis Greenway designed a rebuilt and expanded fort under Governor Macquarie.
Further works were carried out, associated with colonial engineer George Barney.
Most of the fort was demolished to clear ground for the southern approach of the Sydney Harbour Bridge.
Excavated remains of the battery and magazine were opened to the public as an interpretive park.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney, Sydney Barani, State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Dawes Point Battery (National Library of Australia)

properties.name: Dawn Fraser Baths
properties.current_use: Public swimming baths and home of the Balmain Swimming Club
properties.summary: On the Balmain foreshore at White Horse Point, this timber-galleried tidal enclosure opened in 1882 and is regarded as the oldest surviving harbour swimming baths in Australia. It was renamed in 1964 to honour the Olympic swimmer Dawn Fraser, who grew up nearby.

timeline.event: Balmain Council acquired land at White Horse Point in Elkington Park for public baths.
The baths opened on the Parramatta River foreshore, with bathing segregated between men and women by the hour.
The Balmain Swimming Club formed, regarded as probably the oldest swimming club in Australia.
The original enclosure was replaced with a wider 60 yard pool and a two storey changing and viewing pavilion was added to the south.
A north facing changing pavilion and grandstand, two springboards and a tower were added in a major enlargement.
The baths were renamed in honour of Dawn Fraser after she won her third consecutive Olympic 100 metres freestyle gold in Tokyo.
The baths were added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 23 June.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia; NSW State Heritage Register; Inner West Council
properties.image_caption: The Dawn Fraser Baths at Elkington Park, Balmain

properties.name: Fort Denison
properties.original_use: Harbour defence fort and gun battery
properties.current_use: National park island with a tide gauge and weather station, plus a tourist site and function venue
properties.summary: Fort Denison is the small sandstone fort on a harbour island the Eora knew as Mattewanye. The colony used the rock for punishment before George Barney's Martello tower and gun battery were finished in 1857.

timeline.event: Convict Thomas Hill is sentenced to a week on bread and water in irons on the rock, which earns the island the name Pinchgut.
The body of executed murderer Francis Morgan is hung in chains on the island as a warning to arriving ships, and his skeleton remains for years.
Engineer George Barney levels much of the island and begins early defensive works, though the project soon stalls.
Work resumes on the Martello tower and battery amid fears of a Russian attack during the Crimean War.
The fort is completed on 14 November and named after Governor Sir William Denison, and tide measurements begin the same year.
During the Japanese midget submarine raid on Sydney Harbour, shells from the American cruiser USS Chicago strike the fort.
The island passes to the National Parks and Wildlife Service and is opened to visitors as a heritage and tourist site.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Wikipedia; NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service
properties.image_caption: Fort Denison, 'Pinchgut', on its harbour rock

properties.name: Luna Park Sydney
properties.summary: Luna Park opened at Milsons Point in 1935 on the foreshore where steel for the Harbour Bridge had been fabricated. Its grinning Face entrance and Art Deco towers have welcomed crowds onto the harbour ever since, closing only during years of fire, dispute and rebuilding.

timeline.event: Dorman Long sets up a workshop on the Milsons Point foreshore to fabricate and assemble steel for the Sydney Harbour Bridge.
Luna Park opens on 4 October on the former bridge works site, with its smiling Face entrance, two towers and the Coney Island funhouse.
On 9 June the Ghost Train catches fire and seven people die, John Godson with his two sons and four Waverley College students. The park closes.
The site reopens as Harbourside Amusement Park on 29 April after rebuilding.
The Luna Park Site Act of Parliament is passed to protect the site and limit redevelopment.
The park reopens on 4 April after further closures and refurbishment, and has operated since.
Luna Park is added to the NSW State Heritage Register in February.

properties.source: Luna Park Sydney, Dictionary of Sydney, NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: The Face, rebuilt again and again since 1935

properties.name: Macquarie Lighthouse
properties.original_use: Navigational lighthouse marking the entrance to Sydney Harbour
properties.current_use: Working automated lighthouse and heritage site open for guided tours
properties.summary: A light has burned on the South Head cliffs at Vaucluse since 1818, making this the oldest lighthouse site in Australia. The tower standing today was built by Colonial Architect James Barnet in 1883 to replace Francis Greenway's crumbling original.

timeline.event: Governor Lachlan Macquarie laid the foundation stone for the colony's first lighthouse, designed by convict architect Francis Greenway.
The original sandstone tower was lit for the first time on 30 November, the first lighthouse to operate in Australia.
James Barnet completed a near identical replacement tower a few metres away, built because Greenway's soft sandstone had decayed badly.
The two towers stood side by side for a few years before the original Greenway tower was demolished.
The light was converted to electric operation.
The lighthouse was fully automated.
The last resident keepers left the station, ending more than a century of staffed operation.

properties.source: Wikipedia, Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, NSW State Heritage Register
properties.image_caption: Macquarie Lighthouse in 1879, before the tower was rebuilt

properties.name: McIver's Ladies Baths
properties.original_use: Women and children's ocean swimming baths
properties.current_use: Women and children's ocean swimming baths
properties.summary: McIver's Ladies Baths is a seawater rock pool set into the cliffs of Grant Reserve, just south of Coogee Beach. Reserved for women and children, it is widely described as the only continuously operating women's only sea baths in Australia.

timeline.event: The sheltered rock pool, used by local women since the 1830s, is formally recognised and developed by Randwick Council as women's baths.
Council works to deepen and formalise the natural pool are completed.
Robert and Rose McIver take over the operation of the baths, giving the place its lasting name.
The Randwick and Coogee Ladies Amateur Swimming Club takes over the lease and begins running the baths.
The baths are granted an exemption under the Anti-Discrimination Act so they can remain women and children only.
McIver's Baths are added to the NSW State Heritage Register on 18 November.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register; Wikipedia; Randwick City Council
properties.image_caption: The entrance to McIver's Ladies Baths, Coogee

properties.name: North Fort
properties.original_use: Coastal defence battery and command post
properties.current_use: Heritage site and guided tours within North Head Sanctuary
properties.summary: North Fort was cut into the sandstone of North Head in the mid 1930s to guard the seaward approach to Sydney Harbour. Its two 9.2 inch guns and a deep bomb proof plotting room made it a command point for the coastal batteries of Fortress Sydney.

timeline.event: The army began cutting gun emplacements and tunnels into the headland at North Head.
North Fort was established, with two 9.2 inch guns and an underground plotting room linked by tunnels.
During the Second World War the plotting room coordinated coastal batteries ranging from Port Stephens to Port Kembla as part of Fortress Sydney.
The Australian Army School of Artillery opened on the headland and trained gunners there.
The two 9.2 inch guns were removed from the battery.
The Royal Australian Artillery National Museum opened at North Fort.
North Head Sanctuary opened to the public under the Sydney Harbour Federation Trust.

properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust; Australian Army Artillery Museum records
properties.image_caption: A 9.2-inch gun firing at North Head, 1938

properties.name: Prospect Reservoir
properties.original_use: Storage and distribution reservoir for Sydney's water supply
properties.current_use: Service reservoir and settling basin within a heritage and recreation reserve
properties.summary: Prospect Reservoir is the great earthen dam on the western plains that became the heart of Sydney's water supply when water first flowed to the city in 1888. It was the largest dam in Australia at the time and the main distribution point for the city's water for more than a century.

timeline.event: A royal commission was appointed to find a lasting water supply for a growing Sydney, leading to the choice of the Upper Nepean catchment.
Work began on the Upper Nepean Scheme, with weirs at Pheasants Nest and Broughtons Pass feeding a long canal toward Prospect.
Construction of the Prospect dam embankment started, built with a puddle clay core and earth shoulders compacted in thin layers.
The reservoir was completed and water first flowed to Sydney, the dam standing about 26 metres high and over 2.2 kilometres long.
Warragamba Dam was completed upstream, and its water was piped to Prospect for distribution across the city.
The Prospect Water Filtration Plant was commissioned, after which raw water bypassed the reservoir and it became a settling basin and service reservoir.
Prospect Reservoir and its surrounds were listed on the NSW State Heritage Register for their engineering and historical significance.

properties.source: Sydney Water, Engineering Heritage Australia, NSW State Heritage Register, Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Prospect Reservoir and its valve house

properties.name: North Head Quarantine Station
properties.original_use: Quarantine station isolating arrivals from infectious disease
properties.current_use: Hotel, conference and dining complex (Q Station) within Sydney Harbour National Park
