properties.summary: For more than 150 years ships carrying smallpox, typhus, plague and influenza unloaded their passengers at North Head, where arrivals waited out their isolation between hope and the hospital. The station closed in 1984 and the headland is now part of Sydney Harbour National Park.

timeline.event: The Bussorah Merchant becomes the first vessel quarantined at Spring Cove, on the sheltered Manly side of North Head.
Governor Darling's Quarantine Act sets aside the whole of North Head, and the station begins formal operation on 14 August.
A serious smallpox outbreak fills the station and prompts new hospital and accommodation buildings on the site.
Sydney's bubonic plague outbreak sees infected residents and their contacts isolated at the station.
The station handles the Spanish influenza pandemic, and hundreds of returning soldiers and civilians pass through quarantine.
The Quarantine Station closes on 29 February after isolating more than 13,000 people, and the site passes to the National Parks and Wildlife Service.

properties.source: NSW State Heritage Register, NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service, Wikipedia.
properties.image_caption: The Quarantine Station at North Head

properties.name: Spit Bridge
properties.architect: NSW Department of Main Roads (built by Cleveland Bridge and Engineering Company)
properties.original_use: Road crossing over Middle Harbour with a bascule lift span for boats
properties.current_use: Road bridge on Spit Road carrying traffic to the northern beaches, still lifting for tall yachts
properties.summary: The Spit Bridge carries Spit Road across Middle Harbour between Mosman and Seaforth, and its single-leaf bascule span lifts on schedule to let tall-masted yachts through. The lifts cause the weekend tailbacks that have frustrated northern beaches drivers for decades.

timeline.event: A hand-hauled punt began carrying people and goods across the narrows known as the Spit.
A steam punt replaced the hand-hauled service as traffic across Middle Harbour grew.
A low-level timber bridge about 213 metres long opened, built in roughly seven months, and tolls were taken on 62,675 vehicles in the first four weeks.
Work began on the present steel and concrete bridge, with construction slowed by a shortage of steel.
The present bridge opened on 19 November, around 227 metres long with a single-leaf bascule span giving a 24 metre navigation channel.
A proposal to widen the bridge to six lanes was abandoned because of engineering difficulty and rising cost.

properties.source: Wikipedia, Dictionary of Sydney, Mosman Council historical overview
properties.image_caption: The Spit Bridge with its bascule span raised

properties.name: Vaucluse House
properties.architect: George Cookney (additions for W. C. Wentworth)
properties.summary: Vaucluse House grew from a modest stone cottage built by the Irish convict Sir Henry Browne Hayes into the Gothic Revival mansion of William Charles Wentworth, explorer and champion of self-government for New South Wales. It is now one of Australia's earliest house museums, kept with its harbourside grounds and the family mausoleum.

timeline.event: Sir Henry Browne Hayes, an Irish convict, builds a small stone cottage on the Vaucluse estate beside the harbour.
William Charles Wentworth buys the estate from Captain John Piper and begins enlarging the house in the Gothic Revival style.
Architect George Cookney designs the sandstone stables, part of Wentworth's expansion of the estate over the following decades.
Wentworth chairs the select committee that drafts the constitution granting New South Wales self-government, work that carries through to the 1855 constitution.
Wentworth, who died in England in 1872, is honoured with the colony's first state funeral on 6 May and laid to rest in the mausoleum on the estate.
The house and grounds open to the public as Australia's first house museum.
Management passes to the Historic Houses Trust of New South Wales, later operating as Sydney Living Museums.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW (Sydney Living Museums); Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Vaucluse House and its gardens today

properties.name: Victoria Barracks
properties.current_use: Australian Army base and headquarters, with the Army Museum of New South Wales open to the public
properties.summary: Victoria Barracks is a long sandstone garrison on the Paddington ridge above Oxford Street, built in the 1840s to house British soldiers. The barracks has held troops without a break ever since, and the suburb of Paddington grew up around its walls.

timeline.event: Work began on the windswept sand hills along South Head Road under the engineer George Barney, who also designed Fort Denison and the reclamation of Circular Quay.
The main barracks was completed and occupied by British regiments, its sandstone facade running for about 225 metres.
British troops withdrew from Australia and the New South Wales colonial forces took over the barracks.
Soldiers of the New South Wales contingent assembled at the barracks before sailing for the Sudan campaign.
After Federation the barracks passed to the new Commonwealth and became a principal base of the Australian Army.
During the Second World War the barracks served as a busy command and administrative centre for the army in New South Wales.
The District Military Prison of 1847 within the grounds opened as the Army Museum of New South Wales.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney and Army Museum of New South Wales
properties.image_caption: Naval and military officers at Victoria Barracks (State Library of NSW)

properties.name: Warragamba Dam
properties.current_use: Water supply dam for Greater Sydney
properties.summary: Warragamba Dam is a concrete gravity dam on the Warragamba River south-west of Sydney. Its 142 metre wall holds back Lake Burragorang and supplies most of Greater Sydney's drinking water.

timeline.event: Ernest de Burgh, chief engineer in the NSW Public Works Department, first proposed a dam on the Warragamba River.
An emergency weir and pumping station were completed downstream as a stopgap during drought.
Construction of the main dam began under the Metropolitan Water, Sewerage and Drainage Board, with engineer Thomas Upton in charge.
The dam was officially opened on 14 October. The filling of Lake Burragorang drowned the Burragorang Valley and the country of the Gundungurra people.
The dam was listed on the NSW State Heritage Register.
An auxiliary spillway was completed after flood risk studies in the 1980s warned the original spillway was undersized.

properties.source: WaterNSW, NSW State Heritage Register, Wikipedia.

properties.name: White Bay Power Station
properties.original_use: Coal-fired power station supplying Sydney's tram and rail network
properties.summary: White Bay Power Station was built by the NSW Government Railways to power Sydney's growing tram and rail network. It worked for 66 years, survived where its sibling stations were demolished, and reopened to the public in 2024 as the centrepiece of the Bays West renewal.

timeline.event: The NSW Government Railways began construction on the Balmain peninsula to feed the expanding tram and rail network.
The station came fully on line, its start delayed by the First World War.
The last of the 25 hertz equipment was removed as the Sydney tram network wound down, and the station shifted to supplying the wider electricity grid.
White Bay generated its last electricity on Christmas Day, the longest serving of Sydney's metropolitan power stations.
The site was added to the State Heritage Register on 2 April, retaining a rare set of early and mid twentieth century generating machinery.
After a 100 million dollar restoration the building reopened to the public on 9 March, hosting the Biennale of Sydney as the anchor of the Bays West precinct.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney, NSW State Heritage Register, NSW Government (Placemaking NSW)
properties.image_caption: The turbine halls at White Bay

properties.name: Woolloomooloo Finger Wharf
properties.original_use: Wool and cargo wharf, troop embarkation and migrant arrival point
properties.summary: The Woolloomooloo Finger Wharf is a vast timber wharf completed in 1915 for the Sydney Harbour Trust. It shipped wool, sent soldiers off to two world wars, welcomed postwar migrants, and was saved from demolition by a community blockade and union green ban before reopening as a waterfront landmark.

timeline.event: Construction begins for the Sydney Harbour Trust to a design by engineer-in-chief Henry Deane Walsh.
The wharf is completed, becoming the largest of Sydney's finger wharves and one of the largest timber-piled wharves in the world.
A thirty metre extension is added, taking the wharf to roughly four hundred metres long on thousands of timber piles.
Shed No. 7 is converted into a passenger terminal, and the wharf becomes a first landing point for many postwar migrants.
After the government announced demolition, locals blockaded the wharf and unions placed a green ban that halted its destruction.
Rebuilt as a hotel, apartments, restaurants and a marina, the wharf is listed on the NSW State Heritage Register.

properties.source: Wikipedia, NSW State Heritage Register, Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: The Woolloomooloo Finger Wharf

properties.name: Wylie's Baths
properties.current_use: Public ocean baths and community pool
properties.summary: Wylie's Baths is a tidal ocean pool cut into the rock shelf south of Coogee Beach, opened in December 1907. It was one of the first baths in New South Wales to allow men and women to swim together, and it remains one of the oldest surviving sea baths in Australia.

timeline.event: Henry Alexander Wylie, a champion long-distance and underwater swimmer, builds the tidal pool into the Coogee rock shelf. It opens on 12 December 1907 as one of the first mixed-gender public baths in the colony.
Wylie adds the cliff-hugging timber boardwalk. His daughter Mina Wylie, who trained here, wins silver behind Fanny Durack at the Stockholm Olympics, the first Games to admit women swimmers.
After managing the baths for more than fifty years, Wylie relinquishes the lease. The new operator renames the pool Sunstrip.
The baths reopen under the control of Randwick Council and the original Wylie's name is restored.
Architect Keith Cottier leads a major restoration with Randwick City Council, work that later wins a Greenway Award from the Royal Australian Institute of Architects.
Wylie's Baths is added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 14 November.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; NSW State Heritage Register; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Wylie's Baths on the rock shelf south of Coogee

properties.name: Susannah Place
properties.original_use: Four working-class terrace houses with a corner grocery shop
properties.current_use: Historic house museum run by Museums of History NSW
properties.summary: Susannah Place is a row of four brick terrace houses built in The Rocks in 1844 by Irish immigrants Edward and Mary Riley, who named it after their niece Susannah Sterne. The houses sheltered more than a hundred working families until 1990 and are now kept as a museum that shows everyday life left almost exactly as it was found.

timeline.event: Edward and Mary Riley, assisted immigrants from Ireland, build the four houses and a corner shop on Gloucester Street and name the row after their niece Susannah Sterne.
After the outbreak of bubonic plague the government resumes The Rocks under the Darling Harbour Wharves Resumption Act, yet the soundly built houses survive the cleansing and demolitions.
The corner grocery shop at No. 64, which had traded since 1844, finally closes after running for more than ninety years.
With redevelopment threatening The Rocks, residents and the Builders Labourers Federation under Jack Mundey impose Green Bans that halt demolition.
Ellen and Dennis Marshall, the last residents, leave in May after twenty-eight years, ending almost a century and a half of continuous occupation.
The restored houses open to the public as Susannah Place Museum, preserved as found with layers of wallpaper, paint and linoleum left in place.
Susannah Place is added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 10 December.

properties.source: https://mhnsw.au/visit/susannah-place-museum/
